questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with acute decompensated heart failure. His heart rate is 145 bpm. Echocardiography shows a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% with an end-diastolic volume of 90 mL and an end-systolic volume of 63 mL. The attending physician notes that atrial fibrillation at this rapid rate significantly impairs ventricular filling. Which mechanism best explains why tachycardia disproportionately reduces ventricular filling in the setting of atrial fibrillation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tachycardia shortens diastole more than systole, and loss of atrial kick eliminates the compensatory active filling that normally augments ventricular volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tachycardia increases afterload by raising aortic diastolic pressure, which prevents the semilunar valves from closing during relaxation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tachycardia prolongs isovolumetric contraction, reducing the time available for ventricular ejection and subsequent filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of atrial contraction causes retrograde flow through the semilunar valves, reducing the net forward stroke volume significantly

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tachycardia shortens systole more than diastole, but loss of the v wave in the atrial pressure curve prevents passive venous return

explanation.summary: In tachycardia, diastole shortens more than systole, reducing passive filling time. Normally, atrial contraction compensates by providing up to 40% of ventricular filling at high heart rates, but this is lost in atrial fibrillation.
explanation.whyCorrect: When heart rate increases, both systole and diastole shorten, but diastole shortens disproportionately more. At normal resting rates, atrial contraction provides only 10-15% of ventricular filling. However, at maximal heart rates, atrial contraction can provide up to 40% of filling. In atrial fibrillation, the atria cannot contract effectively, eliminating this compensatory mechanism precisely when it is most needed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Tachycardia does not primarily increase afterload by raising aortic diastolic pressure. Additionally, semilunar valves close normally at the end of ventricular ejection regardless of heart rate. The primary issue is reduced filling time, not afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tachycardia does not selectively prolong isovolumetric contraction. All phases shorten with tachycardia, but diastolic phases shorten disproportionately compared to systolic phases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial contraction does not affect semilunar valve function. The semilunar valves separate ventricles from great vessels, not atria from ventricles. Loss of atrial contraction affects atrioventricular filling, not semilunar valve flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tachycardia actually shortens diastole more than systole, not the reverse. Also, the v wave reflects passive venous filling of the atria and is not dependent on atrial contraction; it would still be present in atrial fibrillation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis is undergoing cardiac catheterization. The left ventricular pressure tracing shows a markedly prolonged period during which ventricular pressure is rising but both the aortic and mitral valves remain closed. The peak left ventricular pressure is 240 mmHg while the aortic pressure is 130/80 mmHg. Which heart sound marks the beginning of this prolonged phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: S1, generated by closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves at the onset of ventricular contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: S2, generated by closure of the aortic and pulmonary valves at the onset of ventricular relaxation

choices.label: C
choices.text: S3, generated by rapid filling of a volume-overloaded ventricle during early diastolic filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: S4, generated by atrial contraction against a stiff noncompliant ventricle during late diastolic filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: A systolic ejection click generated by the opening of a stenotic aortic valve during ventricular ejection

explanation.summary: The phase described is isovolumetric contraction, where both AV and semilunar valves are closed while ventricular pressure rises. S1 (closure of mitral and tricuspid valves) marks its beginning.
explanation.whyCorrect: Isovolumetric contraction begins when rising ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure, closing the atrioventricular (mitral and tricuspid) valves, producing S1. During this phase, both AV and semilunar valves are closed, and ventricular pressure rises without volume change. In aortic stenosis, the ventricle must generate much higher pressure to overcome the stenotic valve, prolonging this phase. S1 marks its onset.

whyWrongByChoice.B: S2 is produced by closure of the aortic and pulmonary valves, which marks the end of ventricular ejection and the beginning of isovolumetric relaxation, not isovolumetric contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: S3 occurs during rapid ventricular filling in early diastole and is associated with volume overload states. It does not occur during isovolumetric contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S4 occurs during atrial systole in late diastole when the atria contract against a stiff ventricle. It precedes isovolumetric contraction but does not mark its beginning; S1 does.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A systolic ejection click occurs when the stenotic aortic valve opens, which marks the end of isovolumetric contraction and beginning of ejection, not the beginning of isovolumetric contraction.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman is being evaluated in the cardiac catheterization laboratory. Simultaneous recordings of left ventricular and aortic pressures are obtained. The physician notes a brief period during which blood continues to flow from the ventricle into the aorta despite the aortic pressure exceeding the ventricular pressure. Which property of blood flow best explains this phenomenon during the reduced ventricular ejection phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Frank-Starling mechanism increases contractile force as ventricular volume increases during this phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elastic recoil of the aortic wall generates a pressure gradient that actively pulls blood from the ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: The kinetic energy and inertia of the blood maintains forward flow briefly even when the pressure gradient reverses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Active contraction of smooth muscle in the aortic wall creates a peristaltic wave that draws blood forward

choices.label: E
choices.text: The semilunar valves actively open wider during this phase to compensate for the declining ventricular pressure

explanation.summary: During reduced ventricular ejection, blood continues to leave the ventricle due to kinetic energy and inertia even when aortic pressure briefly exceeds ventricular pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: During reduced ventricular ejection, the ventricles begin to relax and the force of ejection diminishes. Blood still continues to leave the ventricles due to the kinetic energy or inertia of the blood for a short time, even when the pressure in the large arteries exceeds the ventricular pressure. This is a fundamental property of fluid dynamics.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Frank-Starling mechanism relates to the force of contraction based on preload (end-diastolic volume) at the start of systole. It does not explain continued flow during the reduced ejection phase when the ventricle is already partially emptied.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Elastic recoil of the aortic wall stores energy during systole and releases it during diastole to maintain forward flow in the arterial system. However, during reduced ejection, the aortic valve is still open and recoil is not pulling blood from the ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The aorta does not have peristaltic contractions. While the aortic wall contains smooth muscle, it does not actively contract in a peristaltic fashion to propel blood. Forward flow is maintained by elastic recoil and blood momentum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Semilunar valves are passive structures that open and close based on pressure gradients. They do not actively open wider. They close when the pressure gradient fully reverses and flow stops.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with known right-sided heart failure is examined in the clinic. The physician places him at a 45-degree angle and observes prominent pulsations in the neck. The measured jugular venous pressure is 8 cm above the sternal angle. The physician observes the waveform pattern and notes a prominent wave occurring during ventricular systole while the tricuspid valve is closed and venous blood is accumulating in the right atrium. Which component of the jugular venous pressure waveform corresponds to this observation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The a wave, representing the peak in pressure resulting from forceful right atrial contraction during atrial systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: The c wave, representing the brief pressure increase from ventricular contraction bulging the closed tricuspid valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: The x descent, representing the decrease in atrial pressure as the ventricle ejects blood and the atrium relaxes

choices.label: D
choices.text: The v wave, representing the gradual pressure increase from continued venous return against the closed tricuspid valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: The y descent, representing the rapid decrease in atrial pressure when the tricuspid valve opens during early diastole

explanation.summary: The v wave represents passive venous filling of the right atrium during ventricular systole while the tricuspid valve is closed, causing a gradual increase in atrial pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: With the tricuspid valve closed during ventricular systole, venous return continues to fill the right atrium. This causes a slow, gradual increase in atrial pressure known as the v wave. It occurs throughout ventricular contraction and into isovolumetric relaxation. The 'v' stands for venous filling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The a wave represents atrial contraction (atrial systole), which occurs before ventricular systole and before the tricuspid valve closes. It does not occur during ventricular systole with a closed tricuspid valve.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The c wave is a brief, small bump caused by ventricular contraction pushing the closed tricuspid valve back into the atrium during isovolumetric contraction. It is transient, not a gradual accumulation of venous blood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The x descent represents a decrease in atrial pressure from atrial relaxation and ventricular ejection. It shows declining pressure, not the accumulation of blood described in the stem.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The y descent occurs when the tricuspid valve opens during rapid ventricular filling, allowing blood to flow from the atrium into the ventricle. The stem specifies the tricuspid valve is closed, so this cannot be the y descent.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman undergoes an exercise stress test for evaluation of exertional dyspnea. At rest, her heart rate is 70 bpm, end-diastolic volume is 120 mL, and end-systolic volume is 50 mL. During peak exercise, her heart rate increases to 170 bpm, end-diastolic volume is 140 mL, and end-systolic volume is 30 mL. What is the change in her stroke volume from rest to peak exercise, and which cardiac cycle phase is most shortened to accommodate the increased heart rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stroke volume increases by 40 mL; isovolumetric contraction is most shortened as the ventricle contracts faster

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stroke volume increases by 40 mL; diastolic filling phases are most shortened because diastole shortens more than systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stroke volume decreases by 20 mL; rapid ejection is most shortened due to increased afterload during exercise

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stroke volume increases by 20 mL; atrial systole is most shortened since the atria cannot contract at high rates

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stroke volume remains unchanged at 70 mL; all phases shorten equally to accommodate the increased heart rate

explanation.summary: SV at rest = 120-50 = 70 mL; SV at peak = 140-30 = 110 mL; increase of 40 mL. Diastole shortens more than systole during tachycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stroke volume = EDV - ESV. At rest: 120 - 50 = 70 mL. At peak exercise: 140 - 30 = 110 mL. The increase is 40 mL. When heart rate increases, both systole and diastole shorten, but diastole shortens disproportionately more than systole. This means the filling phases (rapid and reduced ventricular filling) are most shortened.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While the stroke volume calculation of 40 mL increase is correct, isovolumetric contraction is not the phase most shortened. Diastolic filling phases shorten disproportionately more than systolic phases during tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The stroke volume actually increases, not decreases. At rest SV = 70 mL, at peak SV = 110 mL. This is an increase of 40 mL, driven by both increased EDV (Frank-Starling) and decreased ESV (enhanced contractility).
whyWrongByChoice.D: The stroke volume increase is 40 mL (110 - 70), not 20 mL. While atrial systole does shorten, the overall diastolic filling period (both rapid and reduced filling phases) is what shortens most substantially.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stroke volume changes from 70 mL to 110 mL, an increase of 40 mL, not unchanged. Additionally, all phases do not shorten equally; diastole shortens disproportionately more than systole.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is undergoing cardiac catheterization. The cardiologist records simultaneous left ventricular and aortic pressure tracings. She notices a small upward deflection in the aortic pressure tracing that occurs immediately after the aortic valve closes. This deflection is sometimes visible as a brief notch in the carotid artery pulse tracing on physical examination. Which phase of the cardiac cycle does this deflection correspond to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial systole, when the atria contract and increase the pressure transmitted retrograde to the aortic root

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid ventricular ejection, when maximal blood flow from the ventricle causes peak pressure in the aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced ventricular ejection, when the declining ventricular pressure creates turbulent flow in the aortic root

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isovolumetric relaxation, when elastic recoil of the aorta against the closed aortic valve produces a pressure rebound

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid ventricular filling, when the opening of the mitral valve causes a transient increase in aortic root pressure

explanation.summary: The dicrotic notch is an upward rebound in the aortic pressure tracing caused by elastic recoil of the aorta against the closed aortic valve, corresponding to isovolumetric relaxation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dicrotic notch is an upward rebound of the aortic pressure tracing that corresponds to a rebound of pressures in the elastic aorta just after closing of the aortic valve. This occurs at the beginning of isovolumetric relaxation, when the aortic and pulmonary valves have just closed. It can sometimes be observed in the carotid artery pulse tracing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial systole occurs during late diastole, well before and temporally distant from the dicrotic notch. Atrial contraction does not produce retrograde pressure changes in the aorta since the aortic valve is closed during diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Rapid ventricular ejection produces the peak of the aortic pressure curve (systolic peak), not the dicrotic notch. The dicrotic notch occurs after ejection is complete and the aortic valve closes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: During reduced ventricular ejection, aortic pressure is declining but the aortic valve is still open. The dicrotic notch occurs specifically after valve closure, not during the ejection phase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rapid ventricular filling involves opening of the mitral valve, not the aortic valve. This phase occurs after isovolumetric relaxation and does not cause pressure changes in the aortic root.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with severe tricuspid regurgitation is examined by a cardiology fellow. On inspection of the jugular venous pulsations, the fellow notices prominent large waves occurring simultaneously with ventricular systole, replacing the normal x descent. The attending explains that regurgitant blood flowing backward through the incompetent tricuspid valve during ventricular contraction produces these giant waves. Which normal waveform component is being replaced and amplified by the regurgitant flow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The a wave, which normally represents the pressure peak from atrial contraction during atrial systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: The c wave, which normally represents the brief pressure increase from the tricuspid valve bulging during isovolumetric contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The v wave, which normally represents gradual venous filling pressure but is now massively augmented by regurgitant systolic flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: The y descent, which normally represents the pressure drop from tricuspid valve opening during rapid ventricular filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: The x descent, which normally represents the pressure drop from atrial relaxation and is now obliterated by systolic backflow

explanation.summary: In tricuspid regurgitation, giant cv waves or prominent v waves occur because regurgitant blood flows back into the right atrium during ventricular systole, massively augmenting the normal v wave.
explanation.whyCorrect: The v wave normally represents the gradual increase in right atrial pressure from venous return during ventricular systole while the tricuspid valve is closed. In tricuspid regurgitation, the incompetent valve allows blood to regurgitate back into the right atrium during ventricular contraction, causing a massive augmentation of the v wave (giant v wave or cv wave). The stem describes waves during ventricular systole, which is when the v wave occurs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The a wave occurs during atrial systole, before ventricular contraction. While giant a waves occur in other conditions (tricuspid stenosis, pulmonary hypertension), the stem specifically describes waves during ventricular systole, which corresponds to the v wave.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The c wave is a brief, small bump during isovolumetric contraction. While tricuspid regurgitation does affect events during systole, the prominent waves described represent a massive augmentation of the v wave rather than just the c wave.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The y descent occurs after the tricuspid valve opens during rapid ventricular filling (diastole). The stem describes events during ventricular systole, not diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While the x descent is indeed obliterated in tricuspid regurgitation, the x descent represents a pressure drop (descent), not a wave. The question asks which waveform is being replaced and amplified; the v wave is the waveform augmented by regurgitant flow.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with long-standing hypertension presents with exertional dyspnea. Echocardiography reveals concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction. His end-diastolic volume is 100 mL and end-systolic volume is 35 mL. Cardiac catheterization shows that left ventricular pressure rises from 8 mmHg to 120 mmHg during a phase in which the ventricle is contracting but no blood is entering or leaving the chamber. During this specific phase, what is the status of the cardiac valves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mitral valve is open and the aortic valve is closed, allowing continued filling from the left atrium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both the mitral and aortic valves are open, permitting simultaneous filling and ejection of blood

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mitral valve is closed and the aortic valve is open, permitting ejection of blood into the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both the mitral and aortic valves are closed, creating a sealed chamber for pressure buildup only

choices.label: E
choices.text: The aortic valve is open and the pulmonary valve is closed, directing all output to the systemic circulation

explanation.summary: During isovolumetric contraction, both the AV valves (mitral/tricuspid) and semilunar valves (aortic/pulmonary) are closed, creating a sealed chamber where pressure rises without volume change.
explanation.whyCorrect: The phase described — ventricular pressure rising with no blood entering or leaving — is isovolumetric contraction. At its onset, rising ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure, closing the mitral valve (producing S1). The aortic valve remains closed because ventricular pressure has not yet exceeded aortic pressure. With both valves closed, the ventricle contracts as a sealed chamber, building pressure without changing volume.

whyWrongByChoice.A: If the mitral valve were open, blood could flow between the atrium and ventricle, and the phase would not be isovolumetric. The mitral valve closes at the beginning of this phase when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Both valves are never simultaneously open during any phase of the normal cardiac cycle. This would create direct flow from atrium through ventricle to aorta, which does not occur physiologically.
whyWrongByChoice.C: If the aortic valve were open, blood would be ejected into the aorta, which would be the ejection phase, not isovolumetric contraction. The aortic valve opens only when ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure, marking the end of isovolumetric contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The aortic and pulmonary valves function on their respective sides of the heart. The left-sided events described involve the mitral and aortic valves. During isovolumetric contraction, both are closed; the pulmonary valve status pertains to the right heart.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman undergoes right heart catheterization for evaluation of pulmonary hypertension. The right atrial pressure tracing shows the normal waveform components. The cardiologist points to a brief pressure increase that occurs just after the a wave descends and correlates it with a specific cardiac cycle event. This small deflection is caused by the right ventricle beginning to contract, which closes the tricuspid valve and briefly pushes it back into the right atrium. During which phase of the cardiac cycle does this deflection occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial systole, when the atria are actively contracting and pushing blood into the ventricles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid ventricular ejection, when the pulmonary valve is open and blood is flowing into the pulmonary artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isovolumetric ventricular contraction, when the ventricle contracts with all valves closed and the tricuspid bulges atrium-ward

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isovolumetric ventricular relaxation, when the ventricle relaxes with all valves closed before the tricuspid opens

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid ventricular filling, when the tricuspid valve opens and blood rushes passively from the atrium into the ventricle

explanation.summary: The c wave on the right atrial pressure tracing occurs during isovolumetric contraction when the contracting ventricle pushes the closed tricuspid valve back into the right atrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The c wave is a slight bump in the atrial pressure tracing caused by ventricular contraction against the closed tricuspid valve. The contraction closes the valve and pushes it slightly back into the right atrium, increasing atrial pressure briefly. This occurs during isovolumetric contraction, when all valves are closed and the ventricle is building pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial systole produces the a wave, not the c wave. During atrial systole, the atria are actively contracting, which is a different event from ventricular contraction pushing on the closed tricuspid valve.
whyWrongByChoice.B: During rapid ventricular ejection, the pulmonary valve is open and blood is flowing out of the ventricle. The x descent, not the c wave, is occurring in the atrial pressure tracing during this phase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: During isovolumetric relaxation, the ventricle is relaxing and pressure is falling. The v wave is completing during this phase. The tricuspid valve does not bulge into the atrium during relaxation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: During rapid ventricular filling, the tricuspid valve is open and blood flows from the atrium into the ventricle. This corresponds to the y descent in the atrial pressure tracing, not the c wave.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with a history of myocardial infarction presents with progressive dyspnea and orthopnea. Physical examination reveals bibasilar crackles, an S3 gallop, and jugular venous distention. His echocardiogram shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 20%, an end-diastolic volume of 250 mL, and an end-systolic volume of 200 mL. Despite the severely reduced ejection fraction, the patient's stroke volume is calculated. What is this patient's stroke volume, and how does it compare to a healthy individual with an EDV of 120 mL and an ESV of 50 mL?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient SV is 50 mL, which is reduced compared to the normal SV of 70 mL, reflecting impaired systolic function

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient SV is 200 mL, which is increased because the dilated ventricle ejects more blood per contraction cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient SV is 70 mL, which is identical to normal because the Frank-Starling mechanism fully compensates for dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient SV is 250 mL, which equals the end-diastolic volume since the entire ventricular content is ejected each beat

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patient SV is 120 mL, which is increased compared to normal because the dilated ventricle has greater preload stretch

explanation.summary: SV = EDV - ESV. Patient: 250 - 200 = 50 mL. Normal: 120 - 50 = 70 mL. The patient's SV is reduced despite a dilated ventricle, reflecting impaired systolic function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stroke volume is calculated as EDV minus ESV. For this patient: 250 - 200 = 50 mL. For a healthy individual: 120 - 50 = 70 mL. Despite having a much larger EDV due to ventricular dilation, the severely reduced ejection fraction (20%) means most of the blood remains in the ventricle after contraction, resulting in a lower stroke volume than normal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: SV is EDV minus ESV (250 - 200 = 50 mL), not 200 mL. The ESV is not the stroke volume. This answer confuses the end-systolic volume with the amount of blood ejected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The patient's SV is 50 mL, not 70 mL. While the Frank-Starling mechanism does attempt to compensate (the increased EDV stretches the ventricle), the severely damaged myocardium cannot generate adequate contractile force, so compensation is incomplete.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The EDV (250 mL) is not the stroke volume. SV = EDV - ESV = 50 mL. The entire ventricular content is never ejected; some blood always remains (ESV). An ejection fraction of 100% is physiologically impossible.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The patient's SV is 50 mL (250 - 200), not 120 mL. While increased preload does stretch the ventricle, the severely impaired contractility prevents adequate ejection, resulting in a reduced, not increased, stroke volume.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with palpitations and lightheadedness. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 150 bpm. The physician explains to a medical student that the P wave on a normal ECG precedes the mechanical event it triggers, because electrical activation precedes mechanical contraction. The student asks which specific relationship between ECG and mechanical events illustrates this concept. Which of the following correctly pairs an ECG event with the mechanical event it most closely precedes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The T wave directly triggers closure of the atrioventricular valves and production of the first heart sound

choices.label: B
choices.text: The QRS complex directly triggers opening of the semilunar valves and the start of rapid ventricular ejection

choices.label: C
choices.text: The P wave precedes the increase in left atrial pressure from atrial contraction, with a slight delay for mechanical coupling

choices.label: D
choices.text: The U wave triggers the rapid ventricular filling phase by causing relaxation of the ventricular papillary muscles

choices.label: E
choices.text: The PR interval directly corresponds to the duration of isovolumetric contraction of the ventricular myocardium

explanation.summary: The ECG impulse precedes the corresponding mechanical event. The P wave (atrial depolarization) starts before the increase in left atrial pressure from atrial contraction, with a slight delay for electromechanical coupling.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that the ECG impulse precedes the change in cardiac pressure. For example, the P wave starts before the increase in left atrial pressure, and the Q wave happens before the increase in LV pressure. This is because actual cardiac muscle contraction lags slightly behind the electrical impulse. The P wave to atrial contraction relationship exemplifies this principle.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization (relaxation), not contraction. AV valve closure (S1) is triggered by ventricular contraction, which follows the QRS complex, not the T wave.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The QRS complex triggers ventricular contraction (depolarization), but this leads first to isovolumetric contraction (all valves closed), not directly to semilunar valve opening. Semilunar valve opening occurs only after ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The U wave is an inconstant finding on ECG whose significance is debated. It does not trigger rapid ventricular filling or papillary muscle relaxation in any established physiological model.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The PR interval represents the time from atrial depolarization to ventricular depolarization (conduction through the AV node). It does not correspond to the duration of isovolumetric contraction, which occurs after the QRS complex.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with right-sided heart failure is being evaluated. His jugular venous pressure is elevated at 10 cm above the sternal angle. The physician examines the jugular venous waveform and identifies a downward deflection that occurs during early diastole, immediately after the tricuspid valve opens and blood flows from the right atrium into the right ventricle. This deflection reflects the rapid decrease in right atrial volume and pressure. Which component of the jugular venous pressure waveform does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The a wave, which represents the pressure increase from right atrial contraction during the atrial systole phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: The x descent, which represents the pressure decrease from atrial relaxation and ventricular ejection during systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: The c wave, which represents the brief pressure increase from the tricuspid valve bulging during isovolumetric contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The v wave, which represents the gradual pressure buildup from venous return while the tricuspid valve is still closed

choices.label: E
choices.text: The y descent, which represents the pressure decrease as blood empties from the right atrium into the right ventricle during filling

explanation.summary: The y descent represents the decrease in right atrial pressure when the tricuspid valve opens and blood flows passively from the atrium into the ventricle during rapid ventricular filling.
explanation.whyCorrect: The y descent occurs when the tricuspid valve opens during the rapid ventricular filling phase of the cardiac cycle. Blood that has been collecting in the right atrium while the tricuspid valve was closed can now passively enter the right ventricle, causing right atrial volume and pressure to decrease. The 'y' mnemonic stands for 'emptYing of the atrium.'

whyWrongByChoice.A: The a wave is an upward deflection (pressure increase), not a downward deflection. It occurs during atrial systole from atrial contraction, not during early diastole after tricuspid valve opening.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The x descent is a downward deflection that occurs during ventricular systole, not early diastole. It results from atrial relaxation and ventricular ejection pulling the base of the heart downward. The timing does not match the stem's description of events after tricuspid valve opening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The c wave is an upward deflection (pressure increase), not a downward deflection. It occurs during isovolumetric contraction, not during early diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The v wave is an upward deflection (gradual pressure increase) from venous return while the tricuspid valve is closed during systole. It does not represent a decrease in pressure after the tricuspid valve opens.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a 20-year history of poorly controlled hypertension presents to the clinic with progressive exertional dyspnea over the past 3 months. His blood pressure is 168/98 mm Hg, heart rate is 78 beats/min, and respiratory rate is 18 breaths/min. On cardiac auscultation, you hear a low-frequency sound immediately preceding S1, producing a cadence reminiscent of the word "Tennessee." An echocardiogram reveals concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with an ejection fraction of 55%. Which of the following best explains the mechanism producing this additional heart sound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid blood flow into a dilated and volume-overloaded ventricle during early diastole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial contraction forcing blood against stiff, noncompliant ventricular walls during late diastole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delayed closure of the pulmonic valve due to increased right ventricular volume during inspiration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turbulent blood flow across a stenotic aortic valve during systolic ejection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tensing of chordae tendineae during passive ventricular filling in a high-cardiac-output state

explanation.summary: The S4 heart sound occurs during late diastole when the atrium contracts (atrial kick) and forces blood into a stiff, hypertrophied ventricle, causing the chordae tendineae to reverberate.
explanation.whyCorrect: S4 is produced when atrial contraction pushes blood into pathologically stiff, noncompliant ventricles (as seen in concentric ventricular hypertrophy from chronic hypertension). The forceful atrial kick against the stiff ventricular walls causes reverberation and produces the characteristic low-frequency S4 sound, heard just before S1 with a 'Tennessee' cadence.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rapid blood flow into a dilated, compliant ventricle during early diastole describes the mechanism of an S3 heart sound, not S4. S3 occurs immediately after S2 with a 'Kentucky' cadence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Delayed closure of the pulmonic valve describes the mechanism of physiologic or wide S2 splitting during inspiration, not an extra heart sound before S1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Turbulent flow across a stenotic valve produces a murmur, which is a prolonged sound distinct from the discrete, short S4 heart sound.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tensing of chordae tendineae during passive filling in high-output states describes a physiologic S3 sound heard in athletes or pregnant women, not S4.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks of gestation presents for a routine prenatal visit. She reports feeling well with no shortness of breath or chest pain. On cardiac auscultation, you detect a low-frequency extra sound occurring immediately after S2. Her vital signs are within normal limits for pregnancy. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial contraction against a ventricle with reduced compliance from gestational cardiomyopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abnormal left-to-right shunting through an undiagnosed atrial septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pathologic volume overload causing ventricular dilation from preeclampsia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Physiologic rapid ventricular filling with tensing of chordae tendineae in a high-cardiac-output state

choices.label: E
choices.text: Paradoxical splitting of S2 from delayed aortic valve closure due to increased systemic resistance

explanation.summary: A physiologic S3 can be heard in pregnant women due to high-cardiac-output states, where rapid ventricular filling causes tensing of the chordae tendineae.
explanation.whyCorrect: S3 gallops are normal in pregnant women, well-trained athletes, and children. In these high-cardiac-output states, the sound represents tensing of the chordae tendineae around the AV valve during rapid ventricular filling in early diastole. This is a physiologic finding that does not indicate pathology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial contraction against a noncompliant ventricle describes the mechanism of S4, not S3. Additionally, gestational cardiomyopathy (peripartum cardiomyopathy) would present with heart failure symptoms, which this patient lacks.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An atrial septal defect would produce a fixed split S2, not an S3 sound. Additionally, ASD would typically present with other clinical findings.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While pathologic S3 can occur with volume overload, this asymptomatic pregnant woman with normal vital signs most likely has a physiologic S3, not one caused by preeclampsia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paradoxical splitting involves the S2 sound splitting during expiration rather than inspiration and is associated with aortic stenosis or left bundle branch block, not pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion and lower extremity edema. Echocardiography reveals severe mitral regurgitation with a dilated left ventricle and an ejection fraction of 35%. On auscultation, you hear an extra heart sound producing a cadence similar to the word "Kentucky." At what point in the cardiac cycle does this additional sound occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During isovolumic contraction, immediately after the QRS complex on electrocardiogram

choices.label: B
choices.text: During late systole, just before closure of the aortic and pulmonic valves

choices.label: C
choices.text: During late diastole, coinciding with atrial contraction just before S1

choices.label: D
choices.text: During early diastole, in the rapid ventricular filling phase immediately after S2

choices.label: E
choices.text: At the end of the T wave, coinciding with ventricular repolarization and semilunar valve closure

explanation.summary: The S3 heart sound occurs during early diastole in the rapid ventricular filling phase, immediately after S2, producing a 'Kentucky' cadence.
explanation.whyCorrect: S3 occurs immediately after S2 during the rapid ventricular filling phase of diastole. In this patient with mitral regurgitation and a dilated ventricle, excess volume enters the ventricle during diastole, exaggerating early filling and producing a prominent S3. The 'Kentucky' cadence (S1-S2-S3 = Ken-tuc-ky) confirms this is an S3.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Isovolumic contraction occurs just after the QRS complex and corresponds to the timing of S1 (closure of AV valves), not the extra sound described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Late systole, before semilunar valve closure, does not correspond to any normal or pathologic heart sound; S2 marks the end of this phase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Late diastole coinciding with atrial contraction describes the timing of S4, which produces a 'Tennessee' cadence, not 'Kentucky.'
whyWrongByChoice.E: The end of the T wave corresponds to the timing of S2, when ventricular repolarization leads to semilunar valve closure, not the extra sound described.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl is brought to her pediatrician for a sports physical. She is asymptomatic and active. On auscultation, the physician notes that S2 appears as two distinct sounds during deep inspiration but is heard as a single sound during expiration. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this auscultatory finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased intrathoracic pressure during inspiration increasing venous return and delaying pulmonic valve closure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed right ventricular depolarization from right bundle branch block prolonging pulmonary valve ejection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left-to-right shunting through an atrial septal defect causing persistent right ventricular volume overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Delayed aortic valve closure from aortic stenosis causing A2 to occur after P2 during expiration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased pulmonary artery pressure forcing the pulmonic valve to remain open longer during both phases of respiration

explanation.summary: Physiologic S2 splitting occurs during inspiration when decreased intrathoracic pressure increases venous return to the right heart, prolonging right ventricular ejection and delaying pulmonic valve (P2) closure.
explanation.whyCorrect: During inspiration, intrathoracic pressure drops, allowing more venous blood to enter the right ventricle. This increased blood volume prolongs ejection across the pulmonary valve and delays P2 relative to A2, producing an audible split. During expiration, A2 and P2 occur nearly simultaneously, producing a single S2. This is a normal physiologic finding.

whyWrongByChoice.C: An atrial septal defect causes a fixed split S2 that remains equally wide during both inspiration and expiration, not a split that resolves with expiration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right bundle branch block delays right ventricular contraction and causes a wide split S2 that is pathologic, typically exaggerated beyond the normal physiologic split.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Delayed aortic valve closure causing A2 after P2 describes paradoxical (reversed) splitting, where splitting occurs during expiration and disappears during inspiration — the opposite of this patient's finding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased pulmonary artery pressure can contribute to wide or fixed splitting, not the normal pattern of splitting only during inspiration with a single S2 during expiration.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known severe aortic stenosis presents for a follow-up evaluation. On auscultation, the physician notes that S2 is split during expiration but becomes a single sound during inspiration. Which of the following best explains this auscultatory pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The stenotic aortic valve delays A2 until after P2 during expiration, while inspiratory delay of P2 causes P2 and A2 to coincide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased venous return during inspiration causes earlier closure of the aortic valve and delayed closure of the pulmonic valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persistent left-to-right shunting maintains equal right ventricular volumes throughout the respiratory cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right bundle branch block delays right ventricular contraction and exaggerates the normal inspiratory splitting of S2

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension forces the pulmonic valve open longer, widening the split during both inspiration and expiration equally

explanation.summary: Paradoxical (reversed) splitting of S2 occurs in severe aortic stenosis because the stiff aortic valve delays A2 until after P2 during expiration; during inspiration, delayed P2 merges with the already-delayed A2.
explanation.whyCorrect: In aortic stenosis, the aortic valve is stiff and takes longer to close, delaying A2 until it occurs after P2 during expiration, producing a split S2. During inspiration, P2 is further delayed by increased right ventricular filling, causing P2 and the delayed A2 to coincide, producing a single S2. This reversal of the normal splitting pattern is called paradoxical splitting.

whyWrongByChoice.B: This describes a mechanism that would exaggerate normal physiologic splitting rather than reverse it. In normal splitting, increased venous return delays P2, not A2.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Persistent left-to-right shunting through an atrial septal defect causes fixed splitting, where the split is equal during both phases of respiration, not paradoxical splitting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Right bundle branch block causes wide splitting of S2 during inspiration, not paradoxical splitting during expiration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary hypertension can contribute to wide splitting but does not cause the reversal of the splitting pattern seen in paradoxical splitting.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is evaluated for a heart murmur discovered during a routine examination. Cardiac auscultation reveals a fixed split S2 that does not vary with respiration. She is otherwise asymptomatic. Which of the following congenital heart defects is most likely responsible for this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting across the interventricular septum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patent ductus arteriosus with continuous flow from aorta to pulmonary artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coarctation of the aorta with increased left ventricular afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with right-to-left shunting and right ventricular outflow obstruction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial septal defect with left-to-right shunting maintaining persistent right ventricular volume overload

explanation.summary: Fixed splitting of S2 is most closely associated with atrial septal defect (ASD), where continuous left-to-right shunting maintains constant right ventricular volume overload regardless of respiratory phase.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ASD, blood flows from the higher-pressure left atrium to the lower-pressure right atrium through the septal defect. The extra blood is pumped into the right ventricle and across the pulmonary valve, persistently delaying P2 closure. This delay remains constant regardless of breathing, producing a fixed split S2 heard equally during both inspiration and expiration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ventricular septal defect causes a systolic murmur due to turbulent flow across the interventricular septum but is not associated with fixed splitting of S2.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Patent ductus arteriosus produces a continuous machinery-like murmur but does not characteristically cause a fixed split S2.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coarctation of the aorta increases left ventricular afterload and may cause a systolic murmur, but it does not produce fixed splitting of S2.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tetralogy of Fallot involves right-to-left shunting and typically produces a single or diminished S2 due to the pulmonic stenosis component, not a fixed split.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 25% presents with worsening dyspnea and bilateral lower extremity edema. On auscultation, an extra heart sound is heard in early diastole. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms best explains this sound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forceful atrial contraction propelling blood against thickened and hypertrophied ventricular walls

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed closure of the pulmonic valve relative to the aortic valve during the inspiratory phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turbulent retrograde flow across an incompetent mitral valve during ventricular systole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid inflow of blood from an overfilled atrium into a dilated, highly compliant ventricle causing wall vibrations

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reversal of the normal A2-P2 sequence due to delayed aortic valve closure from left bundle branch block

explanation.summary: A pathologic S3 in dilated cardiomyopathy results from rapid blood inflow into a dilated, compliant ventricle during early diastole, causing ventricular wall vibrations.
explanation.whyCorrect: In dilated cardiomyopathy, the ventricle becomes enlarged and compliant. During early diastole, blood flows quickly from an overfilled atrium into the enlarged ventricle, causing vibrations of the ventricular wall. This produces the characteristic low-frequency S3 'sloshing' sound, similar to pouring water rapidly into a partially filled balloon.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Forceful atrial contraction against hypertrophied ventricular walls describes the mechanism of S4, which occurs in late diastole just before S1, not in early diastole after S2.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Delayed pulmonic valve closure relative to the aortic valve describes S2 splitting, not an extra heart sound in early diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Turbulent retrograde flow across an incompetent mitral valve produces a systolic murmur of mitral regurgitation, not a discrete diastolic heart sound.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reversal of the A2-P2 sequence describes paradoxical splitting of S2, which is a modification of the S2 sound, not an extra heart sound in early diastole.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man presents with palpitations and mild dyspnea. ECG reveals a right bundle branch block (RBBB). On cardiac auscultation, the physician notes an abnormally wide split S1 and a widened split S2 during inspiration. Which of the following best explains the wide splitting of S2 in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Early closure of the aortic valve due to decreased left ventricular stroke volume from reduced preload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fixed volume overload of the right ventricle from an undiagnosed atrial septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delayed closure of the pulmonic valve due to prolonged right ventricular contraction from delayed depolarization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paradoxical reversal of A2 and P2 timing due to concurrent left ventricular conduction delay

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance causing sustained elevation of pulmonary artery pressure

explanation.summary: In RBBB, delayed right ventricular depolarization prolongs right ventricular contraction, causing the pulmonic valve to close later than normal and producing a wide split S2.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right bundle branch block delays right ventricular depolarization and contraction. Because the right ventricle contracts later, the pulmonary valve closes later, widening the interval between A2 and P2. This produces a wide split S2 that is especially apparent during inspiration, when increased venous return further delays P2.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Early aortic valve closure from reduced preload would not explain the widened S2 split associated with RBBB; the mechanism involves delayed right-sided events, not early left-sided closure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An atrial septal defect produces a fixed split S2 that does not vary with respiration, unlike the wide split in RBBB that varies with the respiratory cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paradoxical splitting occurs with left-sided conduction delays (LBBB) or aortic stenosis, not RBBB. In RBBB, the normal A2-before-P2 sequence is preserved but exaggerated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance from pulmonary hypertension can contribute to wide splitting, but the clinical scenario specifically implicates RBBB as the cause.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a history of left bundle branch block (LBBB) undergoes cardiac auscultation. The examiner notes that S2 is split during expiration but becomes a single sound during inspiration. This patient's S2 splitting pattern is most similar to the pattern expected in which of the following conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial septal defect with persistent left-to-right interatrial shunting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary stenosis with prolonged right ventricular ejection time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe aortic stenosis with delayed closure of a calcified aortic valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary arterial hypertension with increased right ventricular afterload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right bundle branch block with delayed right ventricular depolarization

explanation.summary: Both LBBB and severe aortic stenosis cause paradoxical (reversed) splitting of S2, where splitting occurs during expiration and disappears during inspiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: LBBB delays left ventricular depolarization and contraction, causing delayed aortic valve closure (A2) such that A2 occurs after P2 during expiration. Similarly, in severe aortic stenosis, the stiff aortic valve takes longer to close, also delaying A2 past P2. Both conditions produce paradoxical splitting — S2 split during expiration, single during inspiration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial septal defect produces a fixed split S2 that does not vary with respiration, which is a different pattern from the paradoxical splitting seen in LBBB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary stenosis prolongs right ventricular ejection and delays P2, producing a wide split S2 during inspiration, not paradoxical splitting during expiration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary arterial hypertension delays P2 closure and can cause wide splitting, not the paradoxical pattern where splitting occurs in expiration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right bundle branch block delays right ventricular depolarization and P2, producing wide splitting during inspiration — the opposite of the paradoxical pattern.

questions.stem: A medical student is reviewing the Wiggers diagram and correlating heart sounds with ECG events. She notes that S1 occurs just after the QRS complex and S2 occurs near the end of the T wave. A patient's phonocardiogram shows an extra sound occurring between the T wave and the next P wave. Which of the following heart sounds is most consistent with this timing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: S1, representing closure of the atrioventricular valves at the onset of systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: S2, representing closure of the semilunar valves at the onset of diastole

choices.label: C
choices.text: A systolic ejection click from a bicuspid aortic valve during ventricular contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: S4, occurring during atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle in late diastole

choices.label: E
choices.text: S3, occurring during rapid ventricular filling in early diastole

explanation.summary: S3 occurs in early diastole during rapid ventricular filling, which on the ECG corresponds to the interval between the T wave (end of ventricular repolarization/S2) and the next P wave (atrial depolarization).
explanation.whyCorrect: S3 occurs just after S2 during the rapid ventricular filling phase of early diastole. On the ECG, S2 occurs near the end of the T wave, and the next event is the P wave (atrial depolarization). An extra sound between the T wave and the next P wave falls in early diastole, consistent with S3 timing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: S1 occurs just after the QRS complex, not between the T wave and the next P wave. S1 marks the beginning of systole.
whyWrongByChoice.B: S2 occurs near the end of the T wave itself, marking the onset of diastole, not in the interval after the T wave.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S4 occurs during atrial contraction, which coincides with the P wave on ECG, not in the interval between the T wave and the P wave.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A systolic ejection click would occur during systole (between S1 and S2, i.e., after the QRS complex), not in the diastolic interval described.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic aortic regurgitation presents with progressive heart failure symptoms. Echocardiography shows a markedly dilated left ventricle with an end-diastolic volume of 240 mL (normal ~120 mL). On auscultation, an S3 gallop is appreciated. Which of the following statements best differentiates this patient's pathologic S3 from the S3 heard in a trained endurance athlete?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the athlete, the S3 results from atrial contraction against stiff ventricles, whereas in this patient it results from passive filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the athlete, the S3 is caused by tensing of chordae tendineae during normal filling, whereas in this patient it results from blood sloshing into a dilated ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: In the athlete, the S3 occurs in late diastole before S1, whereas in this patient it occurs immediately after S2

choices.label: D
choices.text: In the athlete, the S3 is caused by delayed pulmonic valve closure, whereas in this patient it is from early mitral valve opening

choices.label: E
choices.text: In the athlete, the S3 represents paradoxical splitting of S2, whereas in this patient it represents a true gallop rhythm

explanation.summary: A physiologic S3 in athletes results from tensing of chordae tendineae during normal rapid ventricular filling, while a pathologic S3 results from blood rapidly entering a dilated, volume-overloaded ventricle causing wall vibrations.
explanation.whyCorrect: In healthy athletes with high cardiac output, S3 represents tensing of the chordae tendineae around the AV valve during normal ventricular filling. In pathologic states like aortic regurgitation with ventricular dilation, S3 results from a large volume of blood rapidly entering a dilated, highly compliant ventricle, causing ventricular wall vibrations — a 'sloshing' sound similar to pouring water rapidly into a partially filled balloon.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial contraction against stiff ventricles describes the mechanism of S4, not S3. Both physiologic and pathologic S3 occur during early diastolic passive filling, not atrial contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both physiologic and pathologic S3 occur at the same point in the cardiac cycle — immediately after S2 in early diastole. An extra sound before S1 would be S4.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Delayed pulmonic valve closure causes S2 splitting, not S3. Neither physiologic nor pathologic S3 involves the pulmonic valve.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paradoxical splitting of S2 is an alteration in the S2 sound pattern, not an S3. Both the athlete's and this patient's extra sound represent S3, occurring at the same point in the cardiac cycle.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension undergoes cardiac auscultation. The physician notes that S2 is split during both expiration and inspiration, but the degree of splitting increases noticeably during inspiration compared to expiration. Which of the following best characterizes this S2 splitting pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physiologic splitting, a normal variant that requires no further evaluation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fixed splitting, indicating the presence of an underlying atrial septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paradoxical splitting, suggesting concurrent aortic stenosis or left bundle branch block

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absent splitting, indicating that A2 and P2 are occurring simultaneously throughout respiration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Wide splitting, caused by delayed pulmonic valve closure from elevated pulmonary artery pressure

explanation.summary: Wide splitting of S2 is characterized by a split present during expiration that increases during inspiration. Pulmonary arterial hypertension delays pulmonic valve closure, producing this pattern.
explanation.whyCorrect: In pulmonary arterial hypertension, high pressure in the pulmonary artery forces the pulmonic valve to stay open longer, delaying its closure (P2). This produces a small split during expiration that widens during inspiration (when increased venous return further delays P2). This is a wide-split S2 pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Normal physiologic splitting produces a single S2 during expiration and a split only during inspiration. The presence of a split during expiration makes this abnormal.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fixed splitting produces an equally wide split during both inspiration and expiration that does not vary with respiration, unlike this patient whose split widens during inspiration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paradoxical splitting occurs when S2 is split during expiration and single during inspiration — the reverse of the normal pattern. This patient's split increases with inspiration, which is not paradoxical.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Absent splitting would mean a single S2 throughout the respiratory cycle. This patient clearly has audible splitting during both phases.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with a history of chronic hypertension presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and lower extremity edema. Echocardiography reveals concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction. Her end-diastolic pressure is significantly elevated despite a normal end-diastolic volume. Which of the following best explains the hemodynamic abnormality in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased ventricular compliance allowing excessive filling at low pressures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased ventricular compliance requiring higher pressures for normal filling volumes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased contractility from chronic sympathetic nervous system activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased afterload from chronic vasodilation of peripheral arterioles

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased preload from excessive sodium and water retention by the kidneys

explanation.summary: In ventricular hypertrophy, thickened muscle walls become less compliant (stiffer), requiring higher filling pressures to achieve normal volumes — representing impaired diastolic function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Concentric left ventricular hypertrophy from chronic hypertension causes the ventricular walls to thicken and become stiff (decreased compliance). A stiff ventricle resists stretching, requiring higher end-diastolic pressures to fill with normal volumes. This elevated filling pressure can backpropagate to the pulmonary vasculature, causing pulmonary congestion and dyspnea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased compliance would allow the ventricle to accept more blood at lower pressures, which is the opposite of this patient's elevated end-diastolic pressure at normal volumes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While sympathetic activation can increase contractility, this patient's problem is impaired diastolic filling from a stiff ventricle, not a contractility issue. Her ejection fraction is preserved.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic hypertension is associated with increased afterload (high systemic vascular resistance), not decreased afterload from vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While excess preload from fluid retention can worsen symptoms, the primary abnormality is the stiff ventricle generating high pressures at normal volumes, indicating a compliance problem.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old competitive cyclist undergoes physiologic testing. During maximal exercise, his heart rate increases from 60 to 180 beats/min. Catecholamine levels are markedly elevated. Which of the following best describes how the elevated catecholamines increase cardiac contractility at the cellular level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Binding to muscarinic receptors on ventricular cardiomyocytes to increase intracellular potassium concentration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activating alpha-1 adrenergic receptors on vascular smooth muscle to increase systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Binding to beta-1 adrenergic receptors on cardiomyocytes to increase intracellular calcium concentration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stimulating parasympathetic nerve terminals to release acetylcholine at ventricular myocardial junctions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing sodium channel conductance in the sinoatrial node to enhance automaticity and depolarization rate

explanation.summary: Catecholamines increase contractility by binding to beta-1 adrenergic receptors on cardiomyocytes, triggering signaling that increases intracellular calcium and enhances actin-myosin cross-bridge cycling.
explanation.whyCorrect: During exercise, the sympathetic nervous system releases norepinephrine, which binds to beta-1 adrenergic receptors on cardiomyocytes. This triggers intracellular signaling that increases cytoplasmic calcium availability, promoting actin-myosin cross-bridge cycling and enhancing contractile force — a positive inotropic effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Muscarinic receptors are activated by the parasympathetic system (acetylcholine), which has minimal effect on ventricular contractility. Potassium concentration changes are not the primary mechanism of inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-1 receptor activation on vascular smooth muscle increases systemic vascular resistance (afterload), not cardiac contractility. This is a vascular effect, not a myocardial one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Parasympathetic stimulation via acetylcholine has only a slight negative inotropic effect limited to the atria, not the ventricles, and does not increase contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sodium channel conductance in the SA node relates to heart rate (chronotropy), not the force of ventricular contraction (inotropy).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision with estimated blood loss of 1.5 liters. His blood pressure is 82/55 mm Hg, heart rate is 128 beats/min, and he appears pale and diaphoretic. Which of the following best describes the effect of his blood loss on stroke volume via the Frank-Starling mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased venous return reduces end-diastolic volume, moving to a lower point on the same Frank-Starling curve with decreased stroke volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased venous return shifts the Frank-Starling curve upward due to compensatory sympathetic activation increasing contractility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased afterload from peripheral vasoconstriction shifts the Frank-Starling curve downward, reducing stroke volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased blood volume increases ventricular compliance, allowing greater filling and maintaining stroke volume

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced blood volume decreases afterload by lowering systemic blood pressure, thereby increasing stroke volume

explanation.summary: Hemorrhage reduces blood volume and venous return, decreasing end-diastolic volume (preload). Per the Frank-Starling law, the heart operates at a lower point on the same curve, producing a decreased stroke volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: Blood loss reduces blood volume, the most clinically significant factor affecting preload. Reduced venous return decreases end-diastolic volume, meaning less ventricular stretch before contraction. According to the Frank-Starling law, decreased preload results in decreased contractile force and lower stroke volume — the heart moves to a lower point on the same Frank-Starling curve without changing intrinsic contractility.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While compensatory sympathetic activation does increase contractility (shifting the curve upward), the question asks specifically about the effect of blood loss via the Frank-Starling mechanism, which involves movement along the curve, not a shift of the curve.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Peripheral vasoconstriction increases afterload but does not shift the Frank-Starling curve. Additionally, afterload effects on stroke volume are separate from the Frank-Starling relationship between preload and stroke volume.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased blood volume does not increase ventricular compliance. Compliance is a property of the ventricular wall. Less filling means reduced preload and lower stroke volume.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While blood pressure may fall, this does not mean afterload reduction increases stroke volume; the primary problem is severely reduced preload from hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with severe calcific aortic stenosis presents with syncope during exertion. His echocardiogram shows a peak aortic valve gradient of 65 mm Hg and an ejection fraction of 40%. Which of the following best explains why his stroke volume is reduced?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The stenotic valve decreases preload by reducing venous return to the left ventricle during diastole

choices.label: B
choices.text: The stenotic valve increases afterload, requiring more time for pressure generation and shortening the ejection phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: The stenotic valve directly decreases myocardial contractility by impairing calcium handling in cardiomyocytes

choices.label: D
choices.text: The stenotic valve increases ventricular compliance, causing the ventricle to dilate and reducing contractile efficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: The stenotic valve reduces heart rate by triggering a vagal reflex, decreasing cardiac output independent of stroke volume

explanation.summary: Aortic stenosis increases afterload by creating resistance to blood exiting the heart during systole. The ventricle must generate higher pressure before the stenotic valve opens, shortening the ejection phase and reducing stroke volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: In aortic stenosis, the calcified and narrowed valve creates resistance to outflow, increasing afterload. The ventricle must generate progressively higher pressure to overcome the valve gradient before ejection can begin. This shortens the ejection phase and slows myocardial fiber shortening, reducing stroke volume. This is consistent with the inverse relationship between afterload and stroke volume.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic stenosis is an outflow obstruction that increases afterload; it does not directly reduce venous return or preload. In fact, preload may increase as blood backs up in the ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aortic stenosis does not directly impair myocardial calcium handling. The reduced stroke volume is a mechanical consequence of increased outflow resistance, not a primary contractility defect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic stenosis typically causes concentric hypertrophy with decreased (not increased) ventricular compliance as the ventricle adapts to chronic pressure overload.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While syncope may involve reflex mechanisms, the primary cause of reduced stroke volume in aortic stenosis is increased afterload from the stenotic valve, not heart rate reduction.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with congestive heart failure is started on digoxin therapy. After several weeks, her ejection fraction improves from 30% to 42% and her symptoms decrease. Her blood volume and blood pressure remain stable. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which digoxin improved her cardiac function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased intracellular calcium in cardiomyocytes, enhancing contractility and stroke volume at the same preload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased preload through enhanced renal sodium and water retention, increasing end-diastolic volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased afterload through vasodilation of systemic arterioles, reducing resistance to ventricular ejection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased ventricular compliance by promoting ventricular remodeling and reducing wall thickness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased heart rate through enhanced parasympathetic tone at the sinoatrial node, prolonging diastolic filling

explanation.summary: Digoxin is a positive inotrope that increases contractility by increasing intracellular calcium in cardiomyocytes, thereby improving stroke volume without changing preload or afterload.
explanation.whyCorrect: Digoxin is an inotrope used to treat heart failure that increases contractility by increasing intracellular calcium concentration. With stable blood volume (unchanged preload) and stable blood pressure (unchanged afterload), the improvement in ejection fraction is attributable to enhanced contractile force, allowing more blood to be ejected per beat.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Digoxin's primary cardiac mechanism is increasing contractility, not vasodilation. The patient's blood pressure is stable, suggesting afterload is unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The patient's blood volume is stable, indicating preload has not changed. Digoxin does not primarily work by increasing sodium and water retention.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digoxin does not increase ventricular compliance or promote beneficial remodeling. Its mechanism is enhanced contractility through increased intracellular calcium.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While digoxin can have parasympathetic effects, the primary mechanism improving ejection fraction in heart failure is its positive inotropic effect on contractility, not heart rate reduction.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man undergoes a pulmonary function test during which he performs a sustained deep inspiration. During this maneuver, intrathoracic pressure drops significantly. Which of the following is the most immediate hemodynamic consequence of this decrease in intrathoracic pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased systemic vascular resistance leading to reduced afterload on the left ventricle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased contractility due to reduced sympathetic stimulation of the myocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased afterload on the right ventricle from elevated pulmonary vascular resistance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased venous return to the right heart due to an enhanced pressure gradient favoring blood flow into the thorax

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased end-diastolic volume from compression of the inferior vena cava by the diaphragm

explanation.summary: Deep inspiration decreases intrathoracic pressure, enhancing the pressure gradient between peripheral veins and the thorax, which increases venous return, preload, and stroke volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: During deep inspiration, intrathoracic pressure drops, increasing the pressure gradient driving venous return (venous pressure minus intrathoracic pressure). This pulls more blood into the thorax and right heart, increasing end-diastolic volume and preload. Per the Frank-Starling law, this increased preload leads to greater stroke volume.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased intrathoracic pressure primarily affects venous return, not systemic vascular resistance. SVR is determined by arteriolar tone, not intrathoracic pressure changes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inspiration does not decrease sympathetic stimulation of the myocardium. Contractility changes require autonomic nervous system modulation, not respiratory pressure changes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Deep inspiration does not acutely increase pulmonary vascular resistance. The expansion of lung tissue actually tends to reduce resistance in extra-alveolar vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Deep inspiration increases (not decreases) end-diastolic volume by enhancing venous return. The diaphragm's descent during inspiration facilitates, rather than impedes, venous blood flow.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with acute decompensated heart failure is admitted to the ICU. Her blood pressure is 220/110 mm Hg, and she is experiencing severe dyspnea with bilateral pulmonary rales. The attending physician starts an afterload-reducing medication. Within 2 hours, her blood pressure decreases to 150/85 mm Hg and her dyspnea improves significantly. Which of the following best explains why reducing afterload improved her cardiac function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lower arterial resistance allows the ventricle to eject more blood per beat, increasing stroke volume and reducing pulmonary congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lower arterial resistance increases venous return, enhancing preload and moving the heart to a higher point on the Frank-Starling curve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lower arterial resistance directly increases intracellular calcium in cardiomyocytes, enhancing contractile force

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lower arterial resistance increases ventricular compliance, allowing the ventricle to fill more easily during diastole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lower arterial resistance decreases heart rate by reducing sympathetic drive, prolonging diastolic filling time

explanation.summary: Reducing afterload (systemic vascular resistance) decreases the resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood, increasing stroke volume and cardiac output while reducing pulmonary congestion.
explanation.whyCorrect: In heart failure with high afterload, the ventricle struggles to eject blood against high arterial resistance. Stroke volume is inversely proportional to afterload. By reducing afterload, the ventricle can eject more blood per beat (increased stroke volume), cardiac output improves, and less blood backs up into the pulmonary circulation, reducing congestion and dyspnea.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Afterload reduction improves forward flow, but its primary benefit is decreasing ejection resistance, not enhancing venous return. Preload changes are secondary, not the primary mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Afterload reduction does not directly affect intracellular calcium or contractility. It reduces the external resistance against which the heart pumps.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Afterload reduction affects the resistance to ejection during systole, not ventricular wall compliance during diastolic filling. These are distinct hemodynamic properties.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While improved cardiac function may secondarily reduce sympathetic drive, the primary mechanism of benefit is reduced ejection resistance increasing stroke volume, not heart rate changes.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic heart failure has a resting heart rate of 82 beats/min and a measured stroke volume of 45 mL. His physician notes that his cardiac output is significantly below normal. If the normal resting cardiac output is approximately 5 liters per minute, which of the following calculations most accurately represents this patient's cardiac output deficit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: His cardiac output is approximately 2.3 L/min, representing a deficit of approximately 2.7 L/min from normal

choices.label: B
choices.text: His cardiac output is approximately 3.7 L/min, representing a deficit of approximately 1.3 L/min from normal

choices.label: C
choices.text: His cardiac output is approximately 4.5 L/min, representing a deficit of approximately 0.5 L/min from normal

choices.label: D
choices.text: His cardiac output is approximately 5.4 L/min, which is actually above the normal resting value

choices.label: E
choices.text: His cardiac output is approximately 6.2 L/min, indicating a hyperdynamic circulatory state
