choices.text: 50%, suggesting borderline systolic function that may indicate early cardiac dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: 65%, suggesting preserved systolic function consistent with an efficiently pumping heart

choices.label: E
choices.text: 80%, suggesting hyperdynamic systolic function consistent with a high-output state

explanation.summary: Ejection fraction = SV/EDV. SV = EDV - ESV = 140 - 70 = 70 mL. EF = 70/140 = 0.50 or 50%. A normal EF is typically >55%, so 50% is borderline and may indicate early cardiac dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: EF = (EDV - ESV)/EDV = (140 - 70)/140 = 70/140 = 50%. This is just below the normal threshold of >55%, suggesting borderline systolic function that may warrant further evaluation for early cardiac dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An EF of 25% would require an ESV of 105 mL with an EDV of 140 mL (SV = 35 mL). The patient's calculated EF is 50%, not 25%.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An EF of 35% would require an ESV of 91 mL with an EDV of 140 mL (SV = 49 mL). The patient's calculated EF is 50%, not 35%.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An EF of 65% would require an ESV of 49 mL with an EDV of 140 mL (SV = 91 mL). The patient's calculated EF is 50%, not 65%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An EF of 80% would require an ESV of 28 mL with an EDV of 140 mL (SV = 112 mL). The patient's calculated EF is 50%, not 80%.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with systolic heart failure is evaluated. His pressure-volume loop shows a significantly narrower width compared to normal, and his ejection fraction is 22%. A cardiology fellow asks about the relationship between stroke work and the PV loop. Which of the following statements about stroke work is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stroke work equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate and is represented by the height of the pressure-volume loop

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stroke work is the area enclosed within the pressure-volume loop and reflects the external work performed by the ventricle per beat

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stroke work is calculated as end-diastolic volume minus end-systolic volume and is represented by the width of the loop

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stroke work equals peak systolic pressure multiplied by end-diastolic volume and is represented by the top of the loop

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stroke work is the pressure difference between the aortic valve opening and closing points and is independent of ventricular volume

explanation.summary: Stroke work is defined as the force exerted by the left ventricle multiplied by the volume ejected, which is equivalent to the area enclosed within the pressure-volume loop. It represents the external work the ventricle performs with each heartbeat.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stroke work equals the area within the PV loop, representing the product of pressure and volume change over the cardiac cycle. In this patient with systolic heart failure and a narrow loop, the enclosed area (stroke work) is reduced, reflecting the ventricle's diminished capacity to perform work.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stroke work is not stroke volume multiplied by heart rate. That product would relate more to cardiac output. Stroke work is the area within the loop, not the height.
whyWrongByChoice.C: EDV minus ESV equals stroke volume, not stroke work. Stroke volume is represented by the width of the loop, while stroke work is the total enclosed area.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stroke work is not simply peak systolic pressure times EDV. It is the integral of pressure over volume change for the entire cardiac cycle, represented by the loop area.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stroke work depends on both pressure and volume changes throughout the cardiac cycle. It cannot be determined from pressure differences alone and is not independent of ventricular volume.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is watching a horror movie when she suddenly becomes terrified. Her sympathetic nervous system activates, causing her heart rate to jump from 72/min to 118/min and her blood pressure to rise. Which of the following most accurately describes the primary effect of the increased heart rate on her left ventricular pressure-volume loop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The loop widens due to increased venous return and greater end-diastolic volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: The loop becomes taller due to the direct positive inotropic effect of tachycardia on the ESPVR

choices.label: C
choices.text: The end-systolic pressure-volume relationship line shifts to the right with increased end-systolic volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: The loop narrows due to reduced diastolic filling time, decreased end-diastolic volume, and reduced stroke volume

choices.label: E
choices.text: The loop remains unchanged because sympathetic activation increases both preload and contractility equally

explanation.summary: An increase in heart rate reduces the time the heart can spend in diastolic filling. This decreases EDV and preload, which consequently reduces stroke volume. The PV loop narrows as the width (stroke volume) decreases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tachycardia shortens diastole, the phase during which the ventricle fills. With less filling time, EDV and preload decrease. By the Frank-Starling mechanism, the reduced preload leads to a reduced stroke volume, causing the PV loop to narrow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tachycardia reduces diastolic filling time, which decreases venous return to the ventricle and EDV. The loop narrows rather than widens.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While tachycardia from sympathetic activation is accompanied by increased contractility (steeper ESPVR), the primary effect of the increased heart rate itself is reduced filling time and decreased EDV.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sympathetic activation increases contractility, which shifts the ESPVR to the left (steeper slope), not to the right. A rightward shift would indicate decreased contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The loop does change. While sympathetic activation increases contractility, the increased heart rate simultaneously reduces filling time and EDV. These effects do not cancel out equally.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with a history of stable angina presents after an episode of chest pain while shoveling snow. His cardiologist explains that the heart's blood supply is unique compared to other organs. Which of the following best explains why the myocardium is particularly vulnerable to ischemia during sustained tachycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The myocardium receives most of its blood supply during systole, and tachycardia disproportionately shortens systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tachycardia increases oxygen extraction from coronary blood, which compensates for reduced flow but eventually causes cellular acidosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary blood flow occurs primarily during diastole, and tachycardia shortens diastole while simultaneously increasing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: D
choices.text: The coronary arteries lack smooth muscle and therefore cannot autoregulate blood flow during periods of increased heart rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tachycardia decreases aortic diastolic pressure, which reduces coronary perfusion pressure but does not affect myocardial oxygen demand

explanation.summary: Coronary blood flow occurs primarily during diastole because ventricular contraction during systole compresses coronary vessels. Tachycardia shortens diastole (the filling and perfusion phase), reducing the time available for coronary blood flow, while simultaneously increasing myocardial oxygen demand through more frequent contractions.
explanation.whyCorrect: During systole, ventricular contraction compresses coronary vessels and open aortic valve leaflets block coronary ostia. Most coronary perfusion occurs during diastole. Tachycardia preferentially shortens diastole, reducing coronary perfusion time while the more frequent contractions increase oxygen consumption—a dangerous double threat.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The myocardium receives most of its blood supply during diastole, not systole. During systole, ventricular contraction compresses coronary vessels and reduces flow.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Myocardial oxygen extraction is already near-maximal at rest (70-80%), leaving very little reserve to increase extraction. The heart must increase blood flow, not extraction, to meet increased demand.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary arteries do contain smooth muscle and do autoregulate blood flow. The myogenic response and metabolic vasodilation are key regulatory mechanisms for coronary flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While diastolic pressure is relevant to coronary perfusion, tachycardia does increase myocardial oxygen demand. The statement that demand is unaffected is incorrect.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease undergoes a pharmacologic adenosine stress test. During adenosine infusion, he develops substernal chest pain and nuclear imaging reveals a reversible perfusion defect in the inferior wall. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the ischemia observed during this test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adenosine directly increases myocardial contractility and oxygen demand, overwhelming the oxygen supply in regions with stenotic arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arterioles in ischemic regions are already maximally dilated and cannot dilate further, while arterioles in healthy regions dilate and divert blood flow away

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adenosine causes vasoconstriction of coronary arteries through alpha-1 adrenergic receptor stimulation, reducing blood flow to all regions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adenosine reduces systemic blood pressure, causing global myocardial hypoperfusion that preferentially affects the inferior wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adenosine closes ATP-sensitive potassium channels in coronary smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction in the territory of diseased vessels

explanation.summary: This describes coronary steal phenomenon. In coronary artery disease, ischemic regions have arterioles that are already maximally dilated at rest to compensate for reduced flow. Adenosine dilates arterioles in healthy regions but cannot further dilate those in ischemic areas, diverting blood flow away from the ischemic territory.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coronary steal occurs because adenosine causes arteriolar dilation. Arterioles in ischemic regions are already maximally dilated due to chronic metabolic vasodilator release. Healthy regions dilate further in response to adenosine, reducing vascular resistance and pulling blood flow away from the ischemic territory, causing a perfusion defect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenosine is a vasodilator, not a positive inotrope. It does not directly increase myocardial contractility. The ischemia results from coronary steal, not increased oxygen demand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adenosine causes vasodilation, not vasoconstriction. Alpha-1 receptor stimulation is a separate mechanism from the sympathetic nervous system and is easily overridden in coronary arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The ischemia is regional (inferior wall), not global, indicating coronary steal rather than systemic hypoperfusion. The perfusion defect is caused by redistribution of flow, not uniform hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosine causes vasodilation, not vasoconstriction. When ATP levels are low, KATP channels open causing smooth muscle hyperpolarization and vasodilation, not constriction.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of myocardial infarction presents with exertional chest pain. His cardiologist explains that the heart has a very high arteriovenous oxygen difference compared to other organs. Which of the following values most closely approximates the percentage of oxygen extracted from blood as it passes through the coronary circulation at rest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 15-20%, similar to the oxygen extraction ratio of renal tissue at rest

choices.label: B
choices.text: 25-30%, similar to the oxygen extraction ratio of resting skeletal muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: 40-50%, approximately double that of the brain during normal waking activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: 70-80%, the highest of any major organ, reflecting continuous cardiac metabolic demand

choices.label: E
choices.text: 90-95%, leaving virtually no oxygen in coronary venous blood under basal conditions

explanation.summary: The myocardium extracts approximately 70-80% of oxygen from arterial blood at rest, the highest arteriovenous oxygen difference of any major organ. This is because the heart is continuously contracting and has extremely high basal oxygen consumption.
explanation.whyCorrect: The myocardium extracts approximately 70-80% of oxygen at rest, significantly higher than skeletal muscle (25-30%). This high extraction ratio means the heart has very little reserve to increase oxygen extraction and must rely primarily on increasing coronary blood flow to meet additional oxygen demands.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 15-20% is too low for myocardial oxygen extraction. The heart extracts far more oxygen than the kidneys, which receive high blood flow for filtration rather than metabolic needs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 25-30% represents the oxygen extraction of resting skeletal muscle, which is significantly lower than the 70-80% extracted by the myocardium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 40-50% underestimates myocardial oxygen extraction. The heart's continuous contractile activity requires extraction rates of 70-80%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90-95% overestimates the extraction. While high, the myocardium extracts approximately 70-80%, leaving coronary venous blood with about 5 mg/dL of oxygen content.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute chest pain. Her blood pressure is 152/82 mm Hg and heart rate is 112/min. Troponin levels are elevated, and ECG shows ST depression in leads V4-V6. The attending physician orders intravenous metoprolol. Which of the following best explains how beta-blocker therapy improves the balance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-blockers decrease heart rate, prolonging diastole and increasing coronary perfusion time while simultaneously reducing myocardial oxygen consumption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-blockers directly vasodilate coronary arteries by stimulating beta-2 receptors, increasing oxygen delivery to ischemic regions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-blockers increase myocardial contractility, improving cardiac output and increasing coronary perfusion pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blockers primarily act by increasing coronary collateral vessel formation over the first several hours of administration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-blockers increase adenosine production in coronary endothelial cells, causing maximal vasodilation of diseased arterioles

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers reduce heart rate, which prolongs diastole—the phase during which most coronary blood flow occurs. They also reduce myocardial oxygen demand by decreasing heart rate, blood pressure (afterload), and contractility, improving the supply-demand balance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-blockers lower heart rate via beta-1 receptor blockade, prolonging diastole and the time available for coronary perfusion. They simultaneously decrease myocardial oxygen demand by reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. This dual benefit improves the myocardial oxygen supply-demand balance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-blockers block beta receptors rather than stimulate them. While beta-2 receptors on coronary vessels mediate vasodilation, beta-blockers would antagonize this effect. The primary benefit is through heart rate reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-blockers decrease contractility (negative inotrope), not increase it. Reduced contractility is actually part of how they decrease myocardial oxygen demand.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Collateral vessel formation (angiogenesis) is a chronic process occurring over weeks to months in response to gradual ischemia, not an acute effect of beta-blocker therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers do not directly increase adenosine production. They reduce myocardial oxygen demand, which actually decreases the metabolic signals for vasodilation.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with chronic stable angina undergoes coronary angiography revealing a 90% stenosis of the left anterior descending artery. His cardiologist explains that despite the severe stenosis, he has not had a myocardial infarction because of an adaptive vascular process. Which of the following best describes this protective mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased alpha-1 adrenergic receptor density in coronary arterioles downstream of the stenosis, maintaining perfusion pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Upregulation of nitric oxide synthase exclusively within the stenotic segment, preventing further plaque progression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shift of myocardial metabolism from aerobic to anaerobic glycolysis, reducing dependence on coronary blood flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autoregulatory increase in systemic blood pressure to maintain coronary perfusion gradient across the stenosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of collateral blood vessels through angiogenesis that partially bypass the stenotic region

explanation.summary: When coronary narrowing occurs slowly and gradually, collateral vessels can form through angiogenesis. These collateral vessels create parallel pathways that partially bypass the stenosis, maintaining some blood flow to the threatened myocardium and preventing infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic, gradual coronary narrowing stimulates angiogenesis and the development of collateral vessels within the myocardium. These collaterals create alternative blood supply routes that partially compensate for reduced flow through the stenotic vessel, protecting the myocardium from infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-1 receptor stimulation causes vasoconstriction, which would reduce flow rather than maintain it. This would worsen ischemia, not protect against it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While nitric oxide is an important vasodilator, the protective mechanism is collateral vessel development, not localized upregulation of NO synthase within the stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The heart relies primarily on aerobic metabolism and has very limited anaerobic capacity. A shift to anaerobic glycolysis produces insufficient ATP and generates lactate, leading to ischemia and pain rather than adaptation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic hypertension is not a protective autoregulatory response to coronary stenosis. While higher perfusion pressure can partially compensate, it simultaneously increases afterload and myocardial oxygen demand.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying coronary blood flow regulation. She notes that when mean arterial pressure is experimentally increased from 90 mm Hg to 130 mm Hg in an isolated heart preparation, coronary blood flow remains relatively constant. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this observation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased nitric oxide release from endothelial cells causes compensatory vasodilation in response to higher perfusion pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased adenosine production from myocardial cells triggers arteriolar dilation to match the higher driving pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial wall smooth muscle constricts in response to stretch from increased pressure, increasing vascular resistance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parasympathetic activation causes cholinergic-mediated vasoconstriction in coronary arterioles to limit excess flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor downregulation in coronary smooth muscle reduces vasodilatory capacity at higher pressures

explanation.summary: Coronary autoregulation maintains stable blood flow despite changes in perfusion pressure between 70-150 mmHg. The myogenic mechanism is responsible: when arterial pressure increases, vessel wall stretch causes smooth muscle to constrict, increasing resistance and keeping flow relatively constant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Autoregulation of coronary blood flow is mediated by the myogenic response. Increased pressure stretches coronary arterial smooth muscle, which responds by constricting, thereby increasing vascular resistance. Since flow = pressure/resistance, the increased resistance offsets the increased pressure, maintaining stable flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nitric oxide causes vasodilation, which would decrease resistance and increase flow. This is the opposite of the autoregulatory response needed to maintain constant flow during increased pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Adenosine is released during metabolic demand or ischemia and causes vasodilation. Increased perfusion pressure does not increase adenosine production, and vasodilation would increase rather than maintain flow.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The parasympathetic nervous system has only a minor effect on coronary vessels, causing slight vasodilation rather than vasoconstriction. Autoregulation is primarily a myogenic, not a neural mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-2 receptor downregulation is not a mechanism of coronary autoregulation. Autoregulation occurs through the myogenic response of vascular smooth muscle, independent of adrenergic receptor changes.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain. Cardiac catheterization reveals complete occlusion of the proximal right coronary artery. The attending explains that the subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemic injury. Which of the following best explains why the subendocardium is more susceptible to ischemia than the epicardium?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The subendocardium experiences greater compressive forces during systole from ventricular chamber pressure and is the last region to receive blood flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: The subendocardium has a lower density of mitochondria and therefore depends more on anaerobic glycolysis for energy production

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epicardial arteries supply the subendocardium first before reaching the epicardium, making the epicardium more susceptible to flow limitation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The subendocardium has fewer adrenergic receptors and therefore cannot mount an adequate vasodilatory response to ischemia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coronary venous drainage is impaired in the subendocardium due to compression by trabecular muscles during the cardiac cycle

explanation.summary: The subendocardium is the innermost layer of the myocardium and experiences the greatest compressive forces from ventricular contraction and chamber pressure during systole. It is also the last region to receive blood flow from the epicardial arteries that penetrate inward. These factors make it the most vulnerable region to ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: During systole, the subendocardial arteries experience greater compression from cardiac contraction and ventricular chamber pressure than epicardial vessels. The subendocardium is the last region to receive blood flow as arteries penetrate from the epicardial surface inward. These flow limitations make it most susceptible to ischemia, especially when perfusion pressure is compromised.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac myocytes throughout the myocardium are rich in mitochondria and depend on aerobic metabolism. The subendocardium does not differ significantly in mitochondrial density from the epicardium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood flows from epicardial arteries through intramural arteries to the subendocardium—the subendocardium receives blood last, not first. The subendocardium, not epicardium, is more susceptible.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The vulnerability of the subendocardium is primarily due to mechanical compression during systole, not differences in adrenergic receptor density or vasodilatory capacity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While venous drainage plays a role in tissue perfusion, the primary reason for subendocardial vulnerability is the greater systolic compression of arterial supply, not impaired venous drainage.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old athlete is performing high-intensity interval training. During peak exercise, his heart rate reaches 185/min and his cardiac output is 25 L/min. A sports medicine physician explains that despite maximal sympathetic activation causing alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction in most vascular beds, coronary blood flow actually increases during exercise. Which of the following best explains this paradox?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary arteries lack alpha-1 adrenergic receptors entirely and are therefore unaffected by sympathetic nervous system activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parasympathetic stimulation of muscarinic receptors on coronary vessels during exercise produces strong vasodilation that overrides sympathetic effects

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-1 adrenergic receptors on coronary artery smooth muscle directly cause vasodilation, counteracting the alpha-1 vasoconstrictor effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: The myogenic autoregulatory response to decreased diastolic pressure during tachycardia produces reflexive coronary vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Local metabolic vasodilators such as adenosine and nitric oxide override the relatively weak alpha-1 vasoconstrictor effect on coronary arteries

explanation.summary: While sympathetic activation stimulates alpha-1 receptors on coronary arteries causing vasoconstriction, this effect is minimal because coronary arteries have few alpha-1 receptors. More importantly, local metabolic vasodilators (adenosine, nitric oxide, low ATP) released during increased cardiac activity override this weak vasoconstrictive effect—a process called functional sympatholysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: During exercise, increased cardiac metabolic activity releases vasodilatory substances including adenosine, nitric oxide, and triggers KATP channel opening from low ATP. These metabolic vasodilators powerfully override the relatively weak alpha-1 vasoconstrictor effect on coronary arteries (functional sympatholysis), ensuring adequate coronary blood flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary arteries do have alpha-1 adrenergic receptors that cause vasoconstriction when stimulated. However, this effect is minimal compared to peripheral arteries and is overridden by local metabolic vasodilators.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Parasympathetic stimulation of muscarinic receptors on coronary vessels produces only slight vasodilation and is a minor effect. During exercise, parasympathetic tone is actually withdrawn, not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-1 receptors on the heart primarily affect contractility and heart rate of cardiomyocytes. Coronary vasodilation is mediated by beta-2 receptors on smaller resistance vessels and by metabolic vasodilators, not beta-1 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The myogenic response causes vasoconstriction in response to increased pressure (stretch), not vasodilation. During exercise, the primary mechanism increasing coronary flow is metabolic vasodilation, not myogenic autoregulation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis presents with exertional angina despite angiographically normal coronary arteries. Which of the following best explains why this patient experiences myocardial ischemia in the absence of coronary artery disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic stenosis causes coronary artery vasospasm through a reflex arc involving aortic baroreceptors

choices.label: B
choices.text: The stenotic valve reduces coronary perfusion pressure while compensatory ventricular hypertrophy increases oxygen demand and compresses intramyocardial vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aortic stenosis increases coronary venous pressure through backward transmission of elevated left ventricular pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: The turbulent flow across the stenotic valve damages coronary endothelium, impairing nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic stenosis causes chronic tachycardia that shortens diastole and reduces coronary perfusion time

explanation.summary: Aortic stenosis creates a multifactorial threat to myocardial oxygen balance. The narrowed valve reduces coronary perfusion pressure while increasing left ventricular workload and oxygen demand. Compensatory left ventricular hypertrophy further increases oxygen requirements while compressing intramyocardial vessels, explaining angina even without coronary artery disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aortic stenosis increases left ventricular workload and oxygen demand while simultaneously reducing coronary perfusion pressure. The compensatory left ventricular hypertrophy increases oxygen requirements and compresses intramyocardial vessels. This combination of increased demand and compromised supply causes myocardial ischemia and angina even without coronary artery disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic stenosis does not cause coronary vasospasm through a baroreceptor reflex. The ischemia results from the combination of increased oxygen demand and reduced perfusion pressure, not vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While elevated LVEDP can reduce the coronary perfusion gradient, the primary mechanism is not backward transmission of venous pressure but rather the combination of reduced perfusion pressure and increased demand from hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Turbulent flow across the aortic valve does not directly damage coronary endothelium. The coronary ostia are located just above the valve, and the mechanism of ischemia is hemodynamic, not endothelial injury.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic stenosis does not typically cause chronic tachycardia as a primary mechanism. The ischemia is caused by the supply-demand mismatch from increased workload, hypertrophy, and reduced perfusion pressure.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man undergoes dobutamine stress echocardiography to evaluate suspected coronary artery disease. At peak dobutamine infusion, a new regional wall motion abnormality is detected in the anterior wall. His cardiologist explains the physiologic basis of this test. Which of the following best describes the principle underlying dobutamine stress testing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dobutamine causes direct coronary vasoconstriction that unmasks flow-limiting stenoses by reducing perfusion to at-risk territories

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dobutamine selectively dilates collateral blood vessels, redirecting flow away from territories supplied by stenotic arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dobutamine decreases coronary perfusion pressure by reducing systemic vascular resistance, revealing areas with compromised autoregulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dobutamine increases cardiac workload and oxygen demand, unmasking regions supplied by stenotic vessels that cannot increase blood flow to meet demand

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dobutamine increases myocardial oxygen extraction ratio above the resting maximum, causing lactic acidosis in regions with borderline perfusion

explanation.summary: Dobutamine stress testing increases cardiac workload through positive inotropic and chronotropic effects (via beta receptor stimulation). This increases myocardial oxygen demand. Regions supplied by stenotic coronary arteries cannot adequately increase blood flow to meet the heightened demand, revealing ischemia as regional wall motion abnormalities.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine is a beta agonist with positive inotropic and chronotropic effects that increases cardiac workload and oxygen demand. This assesses coronary flow reserve: regions perfused by atherosclerotic vessels have minimal ability to dilate and increase flow to meet demand. The resulting ischemia manifests as wall motion abnormalities, unmasking critical coronary stenoses.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dobutamine does not cause direct coronary vasoconstriction. It is a beta agonist that increases cardiac workload. The test unmasks stenoses by increasing demand beyond the supply capacity of diseased vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dobutamine does not selectively dilate collateral vessels. Coronary steal is associated with arteriolar vasodilators like adenosine, not dobutamine. Dobutamine increases workload to unmask flow-limiting stenoses.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While dobutamine may affect systemic hemodynamics, the principle of the test is increasing myocardial oxygen demand to unmask stenoses, not reducing coronary perfusion pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Myocardial oxygen extraction is already near-maximal (70-80%) at rest and cannot meaningfully increase further. The test principle is that stenotic vessels cannot increase flow to match increased demand.

questions.stem: A physiologist is studying the metabolic regulation of coronary blood flow. She observes that when myocardial oxygen consumption increases, coronary arteriolar resistance decreases rapidly. She identifies three key substances primarily responsible for this coronary vasodilation. Which of the following combinations best represents these three primary mediators?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Norepinephrine, angiotensin II, and endothelin-1

choices.label: B
choices.text: Histamine, serotonin, and prostaglandin F2-alpha

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adenosine, nitric oxide, and low ATP activating ATP-sensitive potassium channels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acetylcholine, bradykinin, and atrial natriuretic peptide

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carbon dioxide, hydrogen ions, and potassium ions

explanation.summary: The three primary mediators of metabolic coronary vasodilation are adenosine (a byproduct of ATP breakdown), nitric oxide (produced by endothelial cells in response to shear stress), and decreased ATP levels (which open ATP-sensitive potassium channels, causing smooth muscle hyperpolarization and vasodilation).
explanation.whyCorrect: The source identifies three key factors driving coronary dilation: (1) increased adenosine from ATP breakdown during high energy consumption, (2) increased nitric oxide from endothelial shear stress as cardiac output rises, and (3) decreased ATP opening KATP channels causing smooth muscle hyperpolarization and relaxation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Norepinephrine, angiotensin II, and endothelin-1 are all vasoconstrictors. They do not mediate the metabolic coronary vasodilation observed during increased myocardial oxygen consumption.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While histamine and serotonin can affect vascular tone, they are not the primary mediators of metabolic coronary vasodilation. Prostaglandin F2-alpha is a vasoconstrictor, not a vasodilator.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acetylcholine and bradykinin can cause vasodilation, but they are not the primary mediators of metabolic coronary blood flow regulation. Atrial natriuretic peptide affects renal sodium handling and is not a coronary metabolic vasodilator.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carbon dioxide, hydrogen ions, and potassium are secondary factors that may contribute to coronary vasodilation, but they are not the three primary mediators. The source identifies adenosine, nitric oxide, and low ATP as the key drivers.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history develops atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 162/min during a stressful business meeting. She presents to the emergency department with substernal chest discomfort. Her troponin is mildly elevated at 0.08 ng/mL. Coronary angiography performed the following day reveals no significant coronary artery disease. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which tachycardia alone caused myocardial injury in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tachycardia increased coronary perfusion pressure above the autoregulatory range, causing endothelial damage and microhemorrhage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tachycardia activated the parasympathetic nervous system, causing severe coronary vasospasm through muscarinic receptor stimulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tachycardia caused excessive coronary vasodilation through adenosine overproduction, leading to vascular steal from subendocardial regions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tachycardia increased systemic venous return, raising left ventricular end-diastolic pressure and completely eliminating the coronary perfusion gradient

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid ventricular rate shortened diastole and reduced coronary perfusion time while simultaneously increasing myocardial oxygen demand from more frequent contractions

explanation.summary: Tachycardia causes a dual threat to myocardial oxygen balance. The rapid heart rate shortens diastole, the phase during which most coronary blood flow occurs, reducing oxygen supply. Simultaneously, the more frequent contractions increase myocardial oxygen demand. This supply-demand mismatch can cause ischemia and myocardial injury even without coronary artery disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: At a ventricular rate of 162/min, diastole is markedly shortened, dramatically reducing the time available for coronary perfusion (which occurs primarily during diastole). The increased number of contractions simultaneously raises oxygen demand. This double threat of reduced supply and increased demand causes myocardial ischemia and troponin elevation even in the absence of coronary disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tachycardia does not typically increase coronary perfusion pressure above the autoregulatory range. The issue is reduced diastolic perfusion time and increased demand, not excessive perfusion pressure causing endothelial damage.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tachycardia from atrial fibrillation is not caused by parasympathetic activation, and the parasympathetic system causes only slight vasodilation, not vasospasm, in coronary vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While adenosine is released during increased metabolic demand, the primary mechanism of injury is the shortened diastolic perfusion time and increased oxygen demand, not coronary steal from adenosine overproduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While tachycardia may modestly increase LVEDP, it does not completely eliminate the coronary perfusion gradient. The primary mechanism is shortened diastole reducing perfusion time combined with increased oxygen consumption.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a 15-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with progressive bilateral lower extremity swelling over the past 3 weeks. Laboratory studies show serum albumin of 1.8 g/dL (normal 3.5-5.5 g/dL) and 4+ proteinuria on urinalysis. Renal biopsy reveals diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this patient's peripheral edema?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased capillary oncotic pressure due to urinary loss of albumin reducing the force that draws fluid into capillaries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure due to volume overload from impaired sodium excretion by the kidneys

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure due to accumulation of immunoglobulin complexes in the extravascular space

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure due to loss of structural support from degraded connective tissue matrix

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased capillary permeability coefficient due to direct endothelial damage from chronic hyperglycemia in tissues

explanation.summary: Nephrotic syndrome causes massive proteinuria leading to hypoalbuminemia, which decreases capillary oncotic pressure (πc) and reduces the force pulling fluid back into capillaries, resulting in net fluid movement into the interstitium and edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: In nephrotic syndrome, glomerular damage increases capillary permeability to proteins, allowing albumin to escape into the urine. The resulting hypoalbuminemia decreases capillary oncotic pressure (πc), weakening the force that normally draws fluid back into capillaries at the venular end. Without adequate oncotic counterbalance to hydrostatic pressure, fluid accumulates in the interstitium causing edema.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While volume overload can contribute to edema in nephrotic syndrome through secondary sodium retention, the primary mechanism is the reduction in capillary oncotic pressure from albumin loss, not increased hydrostatic pressure from volume overload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Interstitial oncotic pressure is not significantly increased in nephrotic syndrome. The protein loss occurs through the urine, not into the interstitium. Immunoglobulin deposition in the interstitium is not a recognized mechanism of nephrotic edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure is not a feature of nephrotic syndrome. The interstitial hydrostatic pressure is not significantly altered by glomerular basement membrane disease or connective tissue changes in this condition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While diabetes can cause microvascular damage, the edema in nephrotic syndrome is primarily driven by hypoalbuminemia reducing capillary oncotic pressure, not by changes in the capillary permeability coefficient (Kf) in peripheral tissues.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 25% presents to the emergency department with acute onset dyspnea. On examination, he has bilateral crackles on lung auscultation and pink frothy sputum. Chest radiograph shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Which of the following Starling force changes is the primary driver of this patient's pulmonary findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure in the pulmonary parenchyma from loss of alveolar surfactant integrity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased capillary oncotic pressure in the pulmonary vasculature from hemoconcentration due to third-spacing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased reflection coefficient in pulmonary capillaries from inflammatory cytokine-mediated endothelial damage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure from blood backing up due to left ventricular failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure in lung parenchyma from accumulation of plasma proteins in the alveolar space

explanation.summary: Left ventricular failure causes blood to back up into the pulmonary circulation, increasing pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc), forcing fluid across capillary walls into the pulmonary interstitium and eventually into alveoli, producing pulmonary edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: In left ventricular failure, the heart cannot effectively pump blood forward, causing blood to accumulate in the pulmonary circulation. This raises pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc), which forces fluid across capillary walls into the pulmonary interstitium and eventually into alveoli, manifesting as dyspnea, crackles, and pink frothy sputum.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure is not the mechanism of cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Surfactant dysfunction is associated with ARDS and neonatal respiratory distress, not heart failure-related pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased capillary oncotic pressure would actually favor fluid retention within vessels and oppose edema formation. This would be a protective mechanism, not a cause of pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While inflammatory states can alter the reflection coefficient, this patient's pulmonary edema is caused by cardiogenic mechanisms (elevated hydrostatic pressure), not inflammatory capillary leak or changes in endothelial permeability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure can worsen edema, but it is not the primary driver in cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The initiating event is the elevation in capillary hydrostatic pressure from left ventricular failure.

questions.stem: A researcher measures the following Starling forces at the arteriolar end of a capillary bed: capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc) = 38 mmHg, interstitial hydrostatic pressure (Pi) = 2 mmHg, capillary oncotic pressure (πc) = 25 mmHg, and interstitial oncotic pressure (πi) = 3 mmHg. The reflection coefficient (σ) is 1 and Kf is not provided. What is the net filtration pressure and in which direction does fluid move?

choices.label: A
choices.text: +14 mmHg favoring filtration out of the capillary into the interstitial space

choices.label: B
choices.text: +12 mmHg favoring filtration out of the capillary into the interstitial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: -14 mmHg favoring reabsorption from the interstitial space into the capillary

choices.label: D
choices.text: +36 mmHg favoring filtration out of the capillary into the interstitial space

choices.label: E
choices.text: -12 mmHg favoring reabsorption from the interstitial space into the capillary

explanation.summary: Using the Starling equation: NFP = (Pc - Pi) - σ(πc - πi) = (38 - 2) - 1(25 - 3) = 36 - 22 = +14 mmHg, indicating net filtration out of the capillary.
explanation.whyCorrect: Applying the Starling equation: Jv = (Pc - Pi) - σ(πc - πi) = (38 - 2) - 1(25 - 3) = 36 - 22 = +14 mmHg. A positive value indicates net filtration, meaning fluid moves out of the capillary into the interstitial space, consistent with the arteriolar end where hydrostatic pressure dominates.

whyWrongByChoice.B: +12 mmHg results from an arithmetic error in applying the Starling equation. The correct calculation yields +14 mmHg: (38 - 2) - (25 - 3) = 36 - 22 = 14.
whyWrongByChoice.C: -14 mmHg would indicate reabsorption, but the positive net filtration pressure at the arteriolar end means filtration occurs. This answer reverses the sign of the correct calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: +36 mmHg only accounts for the net hydrostatic pressure gradient (Pc - Pi) without subtracting the oncotic pressure gradient, which opposes filtration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: -12 mmHg is incorrect both in magnitude and direction. The calculation error would arise from misapplying the equation, and the negative sign incorrectly suggests reabsorption at the arteriolar end.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman from sub-Saharan Africa presents with massive swelling of her right lower extremity that has been progressive over several years. The affected limb is firm and does not pit with pressure, and elevation of the limb does not reduce the swelling. She reports a history of recurrent febrile episodes with associated lymphangitis. Which of the following best explains why her edema is non-pitting and unresponsive to elevation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure from chronic right ventricular failure causing venous congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased capillary oncotic pressure from hepatic fibrosis impairing albumin synthesis in the liver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure from tissue fibrosis reducing the elastic recoil of surrounding tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased capillary permeability from recurrent inflammatory episodes damaging endothelial cell junctions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphatic obstruction preventing drainage of interstitial fluid and protein back to the circulation

explanation.summary: This patient has lymphedema due to parasitic filariasis. Lymphatic obstruction prevents drainage of excess interstitial fluid and proteins, causing non-pitting edema that does not respond to elevation or diuretics.
explanation.whyCorrect: Filariasis causes lymphatic obstruction by parasitic infection of lymph nodes. When lymphatics are damaged, even normal amounts of filtered fluid cannot return to circulation and accumulate in the affected limb. The resulting lymphedema is characteristically non-pitting because accumulated proteins cause tissue fibrosis, and it does not respond to elevation or diuretics because the drainage pathway itself is obstructed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right ventricular failure causes bilateral dependent pitting edema from elevated capillary hydrostatic pressure, not the unilateral non-pitting edema seen in this patient. Cardiac edema typically responds to elevation and diuretics.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypoalbuminemia from liver disease causes generalized pitting edema and ascites, not the localized non-pitting edema of a single extremity seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure would actually favor filtration of fluid out of capillaries. Tissue fibrosis is a consequence of chronic lymphedema, not the primary cause of the edema pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While recurrent inflammation occurs in filariasis, increased capillary permeability alone would cause pitting edema that could improve with elevation. The non-pitting, elevation-resistant nature points to lymphatic obstruction as the primary mechanism.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to a humanitarian clinic in a refugee camp. He has been receiving adequate caloric intake from rice and cassava but minimal protein intake for the past year. Physical examination reveals a distended abdomen, generalized edema, and an enlarged fatty liver. His serum albumin is 1.2 g/dL. Which of the following changes in Starling forces most directly explains his clinical presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure from fluid overload secondary to impaired cardiac contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased interstitial hydrostatic pressure from excess dietary carbohydrate causing fluid retention in tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased capillary permeability coefficient from hepatic dysfunction reducing blood flow to peripheral tissues

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure from breakdown of intracellular proteins leaking into the extracellular space

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased capillary oncotic pressure from inadequate protein intake impairing hepatic albumin synthesis

explanation.summary: This child has kwashiorkor from protein malnutrition. Insufficient protein intake impairs hepatic albumin synthesis, decreasing capillary oncotic pressure (πc), which reduces the force pulling fluid back into capillaries, resulting in generalized edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: In kwashiorkor, inadequate protein intake means the liver cannot synthesize sufficient albumin. As plasma albumin falls, capillary oncotic pressure (πc) decreases. This weakens the inward pull that normally draws fluid from the interstitium back into capillaries. With reduced oncotic counterbalance to capillary hydrostatic pressure, fluid accumulates in the interstitium causing the generalized edema characteristic of kwashiorkor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure occurs in heart failure, not kwashiorkor. While the child may have some fluid shifts, the primary problem is protein deficiency reducing oncotic pressure, not cardiac pump failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased interstitial hydrostatic pressure would actually oppose filtration and favor fluid movement back into capillaries. Dietary carbohydrate excess does not directly increase interstitial hydrostatic pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The capillary permeability coefficient (Kf) is not decreased in kwashiorkor. Moreover, decreased Kf would actually reduce fluid movement across capillaries, which would not explain the edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure from intracellular protein breakdown is not the primary mechanism in kwashiorkor. The key issue is insufficient plasma albumin synthesis, not protein leakage into the interstitium.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis presents with increasing abdominal girth and bilateral lower extremity swelling. Paracentesis yields 3 liters of transudative ascitic fluid. His serum albumin is 2.0 g/dL. A medical student asks which body fluid compartments are governed by Starling forces. Which of the following is the most accurate response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the intracellular and extracellular compartments across cell membranes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the intravascular and interstitial spaces across capillary walls

choices.label: C
choices.text: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the intracellular and intravascular compartments via active transport

choices.label: D
choices.text: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the cytoplasm and the nucleus across the nuclear membrane envelope

choices.label: E
choices.text: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the intracellular and lymphatic compartments through tissue matrices

explanation.summary: Starling forces govern fluid movement between the intravascular (plasma) and interstitial spaces, both of which are extracellular compartments. They do not control fluid shifts between intracellular and extracellular compartments.
explanation.whyCorrect: Starling forces (hydrostatic and oncotic pressures) control fluid exchange between the intravascular space (plasma within capillaries) and the interstitial space (fluid between cells). Both are extracellular fluid compartments. Fluid movement between intracellular and extracellular compartments is governed by osmosis and tonicity, not Starling forces.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fluid movement between intracellular and extracellular compartments is governed by osmosis and tonicity across cell membranes, not by Starling forces. Starling forces specifically apply to capillary-interstitial fluid exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no direct Starling force-mediated exchange between intracellular and intravascular compartments. Additionally, active transport is a cellular mechanism unrelated to Starling forces.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fluid movement across the nuclear membrane is an intracellular process governed by nuclear pore complexes, not by Starling forces which operate at the capillary-tissue interface.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Starling forces do not govern movement between intracellular fluid and lymphatics. Lymphatics drain excess interstitial fluid, but the forces driving this involve interstitial pressure gradients, not classic Starling forces across cell membranes.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman sustains extensive second and third degree burns over 40% of her total body surface area. Within 6 hours of injury, she develops severe generalized edema requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation. Which of the following changes in capillary properties best explains the rapid onset of edema in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decrease in capillary hydrostatic pressure from reduced cardiac output due to hypovolemic shock

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase in capillary oncotic pressure from hemoconcentration caused by plasma volume depletion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decrease in interstitial oncotic pressure from dilution of interstitial proteins by excess lymphatic flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alteration of the reflection coefficient from injury-induced changes in capillary wall permeability

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decrease in capillary permeability coefficient from endothelial swelling occluding intercellular junctions

explanation.summary: Burns cause injury-mediated release of inflammatory mediators that alter the reflection coefficient (σ) by increasing capillary wall permeability, allowing proteins and fluid to leak into the interstitium, producing rapid onset edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Burn injury releases inflammatory cytokines and mediators that damage capillary endothelium, altering the reflection coefficient (σ). A decreased σ means more protein can leak through the capillary wall, which increases interstitial oncotic pressure and reduces effective capillary oncotic pressure. This shifts the balance of Starling forces toward filtration, causing rapid fluid accumulation in the interstitium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While cardiac output may decrease later from hypovolemia, decreased capillary hydrostatic pressure would actually reduce filtration and oppose edema formation, not promote it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased capillary oncotic pressure from hemoconcentration would favor fluid retention within vessels, opposing edema. This is a compensatory response, not a cause of the edema.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased interstitial oncotic pressure would favor fluid movement back into capillaries, opposing edema formation. In burns, interstitial oncotic pressure actually increases as proteins leak out of damaged capillaries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased capillary permeability would reduce fluid movement across capillary walls. Burns increase permeability through endothelial damage, not decrease it through endothelial swelling.

questions.stem: A physiology student is studying the microcirculation and measures Starling forces at the venular end of a capillary: Pc = 17 mmHg, Pi = 1 mmHg, πc = 26 mmHg, πi = 1 mmHg. Assuming σ = 1 and Kf is omitted, the student calculates the net flux (Jv). Which of the following best describes the result and the physiological reason for the change in capillary hydrostatic pressure from the arteriolar end?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Jv = +9 mmHg; hydrostatic pressure increased because smooth muscle contraction at the venular end raises resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Jv = -9 mmHg; hydrostatic pressure fell because fluid was filtered out at the arteriolar end, reducing intravascular volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Jv = -9 mmHg; hydrostatic pressure fell because the venular end is farther from the heart and has lower cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Jv = +9 mmHg; hydrostatic pressure remained the same as the arteriolar end since oncotic pressure is the only variable

choices.label: E
choices.text: Jv = 0 mmHg; hydrostatic and oncotic forces are perfectly balanced at the venular end so no net fluid movement occurs

explanation.summary: Jv = (17 - 1) - (26 - 1) = 16 - 25 = -9 mmHg, indicating reabsorption. Capillary hydrostatic pressure drops from the arteriolar to venular end because fluid was filtered out proximally, reducing intravascular volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: At the venular end, Jv = (Pc - Pi) - σ(πc - πi) = (17 - 1) - 1(26 - 1) = 16 - 25 = -9 mmHg. The negative value indicates reabsorption. The hydrostatic pressure dropped from approximately 36 mmHg at the arteriolar end to 17 mmHg at the venular end because fluid was filtered out at the arteriolar end, reducing the volume of fluid within the capillary and thus the hydrostatic pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Jv is -9, not +9. Additionally, the hydrostatic pressure decreases along the capillary, it does not increase. Smooth muscle contraction is minimal in capillaries, which lack significant smooth muscle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While the calculation of -9 is correct, the explanation is wrong. Capillary hydrostatic pressure falls because fluid was filtered out at the arteriolar end, not because of distance from the heart or changes in cardiac output. Cardiac output is the same throughout the circulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Jv is -9, not +9, and the hydrostatic pressure does change significantly along the capillary length. Both hydrostatic and oncotic pressures contribute to net flux, but hydrostatic pressure decreases while oncotic pressure remains relatively constant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The forces are not balanced at the venular end. The calculation clearly shows a net pressure of -9 mmHg favoring reabsorption, as oncotic pressure exceeds hydrostatic pressure at this location.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with chronic right-sided heart failure presents with worsening bilateral ankle edema that has progressed proximally to his thighs over the past month. Examination reveals 3+ pitting edema bilaterally. Skin over the lower extremities shows hyperpigmentation and early ulceration. Which of the following best explains why his edema has become clinically significant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess filtration has overwhelmed lymphatic drainage capacity, causing fluid accumulation that impairs cellular nutrition by increasing diffusion distances

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased interstitial oncotic pressure has reduced fluid retention in the extravascular space, paradoxically worsening venous congestion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased capillary oncotic pressure from polycythemia has shifted fluid into the intravascular space, reducing tissue perfusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymphatic obstruction from chronic venous hypertension has replaced the normal capacitance function of the venous system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased capillary permeability from chronic pressure elevation has reduced nutrient delivery across the capillary endothelium

explanation.summary: Right heart failure increases systemic venous pressure, which backs up into capillaries raising capillary hydrostatic pressure. Excess filtration overwhelms lymphatic capacity, and the resulting interstitial fluid accumulation impairs nutrition by increasing diffusion distances, leading to skin changes and ulceration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In right heart failure, blood backs up into the systemic circulation, elevating peripheral capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc). This increases filtration, exceeding the lymphatic system's capacity to remove interstitial fluid (normally 2-4 L/day). The resulting tissue edema impairs cellular nutrition by increasing diffusion distances between capillaries and cells, leading to skin ulceration, blister formation, and increased susceptibility to infection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased interstitial oncotic pressure would actually favor fluid movement back into capillaries, opposing edema. This does not explain the worsening edema or venous congestion seen in right heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased capillary oncotic pressure would draw fluid into vessels, not cause tissue edema. Polycythemia is not a feature of right heart failure, and fluid shifts into the intravascular space would not cause peripheral edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While chronic edema can eventually impair lymphatics, the primary mechanism in right heart failure is elevated capillary hydrostatic pressure overwhelming normal lymphatic drainage capacity, not primary lymphatic obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased capillary permeability would reduce, not increase, filtration. Chronic venous hypertension does not decrease permeability; if anything, chronic inflammation may increase it.

questions.stem: A researcher is comparing capillary types across different organs. She notes that hepatic sinusoidal capillaries have large pores and discontinuities in their walls, while skeletal muscle capillaries have a continuous endothelial lining. Which of the following best describes how these structural differences affect the Starling equation parameters?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sinusoidal capillaries have a higher reflection coefficient than continuous capillaries, preventing protein leakage and maintaining oncotic gradients

choices.label: B
choices.text: Continuous capillaries have a higher capillary permeability coefficient than sinusoidal capillaries due to their tighter intercellular junctions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continuous capillaries have a lower reflection coefficient than sinusoidal capillaries because their tight junctions allow selective protein transport

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinusoidal capillaries have a higher capillary permeability coefficient and lower reflection coefficient, allowing greater fluid and protein movement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both capillary types have identical permeability and reflection coefficients since the Starling equation parameters are fixed across all tissues

explanation.summary: Sinusoidal (discontinuous) capillaries have large pores, giving them a higher Kf (more fluid movement) and lower σ (more protein leakage) compared to continuous capillaries which have tight endothelial junctions restricting both fluid and protein passage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Discontinuous/sinusoidal capillaries (found in liver, spleen, bone marrow) have large pores and gaps in their walls, resulting in a higher capillary permeability coefficient (Kf), meaning more fluid moves across for a given pressure gradient. They also have a lower reflection coefficient (σ), meaning proteins like albumin can more easily leak through. This is functionally important since the liver produces albumin and needs to export it into the circulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sinusoidal capillaries have a lower, not higher, reflection coefficient. Their large pores allow proteins to cross freely, which is essential for organs like the liver that synthesize and export large plasma proteins.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Continuous capillaries have a lower, not higher, Kf compared to sinusoidal capillaries. The continuous endothelial lining restricts fluid movement, while sinusoidal capillaries with large pores are far more permeable.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Continuous capillaries have a higher, not lower, reflection coefficient. Their tight junctions prevent protein leakage, maintaining the oncotic pressure gradient that is important for fluid balance in peripheral tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The permeability coefficient and reflection coefficient vary significantly across different capillary types and organs. These parameters are not fixed and can also change during disease, development, or injury.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman undergoes axillary lymph node dissection as part of breast cancer treatment. Six months later, she develops progressive swelling of the ipsilateral arm. Physical therapy and compression garments provide minimal improvement. The lymphatic system normally drains approximately 2-4 liters of fluid per day. Which of the following best explains why the lymphatic fluid ultimately returns to the systemic circulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lymphatic fluid drains into the hepatic portal vein through connections between mesenteric lymphatics and portal tributaries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lymphatic fluid is reabsorbed directly into capillary venules through osmotic pressure gradients in the peripheral tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lymphatic fluid drains into the thoracic duct, which empties into the left subclavian vein to rejoin the venous circulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymphatic fluid enters the right atrium through dedicated lymphatic channels that bypass the systemic venous system entirely

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphatic fluid is filtered through the kidneys and excreted as dilute urine before being replaced by new capillary filtrate

explanation.summary: The lymphatic system collects excess interstitial fluid and drains it through the thoracic duct into the left subclavian vein, returning it to the systemic venous circulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The lymphatic system drains excess interstitial fluid (2-4 L/day that is not reabsorbed at the venular end of capillaries), along with proteins and bacteria, through progressively larger lymphatic vessels. This fluid ultimately reaches the thoracic duct, which drains into the left subclavian vein, returning the fluid to the venous circulation and maintaining fluid balance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lymphatic fluid does not drain into the hepatic portal vein. While intestinal lymphatics (lacteals) absorb dietary lipids, the lymphatic system as a whole drains into the venous system via the thoracic duct and right lymphatic duct.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lymphatic fluid is not reabsorbed back into capillary venules. The lymphatic system is a separate drainage pathway that collects fluid the venular reabsorption process missed, and returns it to the venous circulation via the thoracic duct.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There are no dedicated lymphatic channels that bypass the venous system to reach the right atrium directly. Lymph returns to the circulation by draining into the subclavian veins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymphatic fluid is not filtered through the kidneys. It returns to the venous circulation via the thoracic duct. The kidneys filter blood plasma, not lymphatic fluid directly.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with decompensated biventricular heart failure and nephrotic syndrome from diabetic nephropathy presents with anasarca. His serum albumin is 1.5 g/dL and his jugular venous pressure is markedly elevated. Which of the following combinations of Starling force changes most accurately explains the severity of this patient's edema?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased capillary hydrostatic pressure and increased capillary oncotic pressure both favoring interstitial fluid reabsorption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure and decreased capillary oncotic pressure both favoring fluid movement into the interstitium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure and increased interstitial oncotic pressure both favoring fluid retention in capillaries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased capillary oncotic pressure and decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure both opposing fluid filtration from capillaries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased capillary hydrostatic pressure and increased interstitial oncotic pressure both favoring fluid accumulation in the interstitium

explanation.summary: Biventricular heart failure increases capillary hydrostatic pressure (blood backs up), while nephrotic syndrome decreases capillary oncotic pressure (albumin lost in urine). Both forces shift toward net filtration, explaining the severity of anasarca.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has two simultaneous disruptions of Starling forces. Heart failure causes blood to back up, increasing capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc), which pushes more fluid out of capillaries. Nephrotic syndrome causes albumin loss in urine, decreasing capillary oncotic pressure (πc), which reduces the force pulling fluid back into capillaries. Together, these changes dramatically increase net filtration, explaining the severity of his anasarca.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both of these changes would favor fluid retention within vessels and oppose edema. Heart failure increases, not decreases, capillary hydrostatic pressure. Nephrotic syndrome decreases, not increases, capillary oncotic pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased interstitial hydrostatic pressure and increased interstitial oncotic pressure would both favor fluid leaving capillaries, not retention. Moreover, these are not the primary Starling force changes in heart failure and nephrotic syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Capillary oncotic pressure is decreased, not increased, in nephrotic syndrome. These proposed changes would oppose filtration and edema, which contradicts the patient's clinical presentation of anasarca.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Capillary hydrostatic pressure is increased, not decreased, in heart failure due to blood backing up. While increased interstitial oncotic pressure could contribute to edema, the primary changes are elevated Pc and decreased πc.
